planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; seeds are very small and should be pressed onto the soil surface without covering.

directions: Alpine strawberries are compact, clumping, runner-free plants that produce small but intensely fragrant and flavored berries continuously from early summer through fall frost. Seeds are tiny and require light for germination — surface sow in trays and do not cover; keep moist at 65–75°F. Unlike garden strawberries, alpines form clumps rather than runners and are best propagated by division every 3–4 years or from seed. They are more shade-tolerant than hybrid strawberries, making them excellent for edging partially shaded paths. Harvest individual berries when fully red and aromatic.

planting.note: Plant one male per 4–6 female vines after last frost; protect new shoots from cats, which are strongly attracted to kolomikta vines.

directions: Actinidia kolomikta is the hardiest kiwi species, tolerating temperatures to -40°F in zone 3, and is grown for its small (1 in), sweet, smooth-skinned fruits that ripen in late summer. The vine is ornamentally striking due to pink-and-white variegated foliage on male plants. It is dioecious and requires both male and female plants — one male per 4–6 females. Cats are intensely attracted to the plants and will damage young vines, so protect new plantings with wire cages. Prune each dormant season to maintain a framework of lateral arms on a sturdy arbor or fence.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; extremely adaptable to wet or dry soils.

directions: Aronia is exceptionally hardy and tolerates wet, poorly drained soils, making it suitable for rain gardens. It is self-fruitful and rarely needs a pollinator, though yields improve with a second plant nearby. Prune by removing the oldest, thickest canes at the base every 3–4 years to encourage vigorous new growth and better light penetration. Harvest clusters in late summer when berries are very dark purple-black; the astringent flavor mellows after a light frost or processing.

planting.note: Plant in spring in any well-drained soil; autumn olive is highly invasive in the eastern U.S. — confirm it is legal in your state before planting.

directions: Autumn olive is a nitrogen-fixing Asian shrub with extremely high yields of small red-speckled berries that are rich in lycopene and have a sweet-tart flavor; it is among the most productive fruiting shrubs per acre in temperate climates. However, it is considered an invasive species across much of the eastern U.S. and is prohibited or discouraged in many states — verify legal status in your jurisdiction before planting. Named fruit-producing selections such as 'Garnet' or 'Red Wing' are more compact and productive than seedlings. Harvest by hand-stripping clusters in fall when berries are fully red; fruit quality improves after light frost.

planting.note: Plant two or more named cultivars for cross-pollination in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil.

directions: Beach plum is a native Atlantic coastal shrub that thrives in sandy, salt-spray-exposed sites where few other fruits survive, making it invaluable for coastal and xeric gardens. The small (0.5–1 in), round fruits range from red to purple to blue at maturity in late summer and are traditionally used for preserves and jams on Cape Cod and Long Island. Plant at least two named cultivars such as 'Autumn' and 'Premier' for adequate cross-pollination, as seedlings are variable and often infertile. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape; heavy pruning reduces fruiting the following year.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring in strongly acidic, humus-rich, moist soil, ideally under light tree canopy.

directions: Bilberry is a low-growing European relative of blueberry that differs in producing berries singly rather than in clusters; the deep purple-red flesh (unlike blueberry's white flesh) stains heavily and has an intense, slightly tart flavor. It demands strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0) with high organic matter and cool, moist conditions, making it challenging to grow in most American gardens but worth attempting in zones 3–7 under high shade or at elevation. Minimal pruning is needed; shear plants lightly every 3–4 years to rejuvenate. Harvest in mid- to late summer when berries are fully dark blue-black and tender.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or dormant bare-root stock in early spring, burying the cutting deeper than other Ribes to encourage multiple basal shoots.

directions: Black currants fruit most heavily on one-year-old wood, so annual renewal pruning is critical: remove one-third of the oldest canes each late winter to stimulate vigorous new shoots. The berries have a strong, tannic flavor prized for jams and juices; harvest when the entire strig has turned glossy black. Choose blister-rust-resistant cultivars such as 'Consort' or 'Titania' where regulations permit. Verify local restrictions before planting.

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; keep well away from other Rubus species to limit virus transmission.

directions: Black raspberries are erect, arching canes that propagate by tip layering rather than suckering; they have a richer, more intense flavor than red raspberries and are popular for jams and wines. Tip-prune primocanes at 24–30 in in midsummer to force lateral branching, then shorten those laterals to 8–12 in the following late winter for maximum fruit bud development. Remove all floricanes after harvest and any tips that have rooted outside the row. Do not plant near red raspberries or wild Rubus, as black raspberries are highly susceptible to aphid-spread viruses.

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; trailing and semi-erect types need a trellis installed at planting.

directions: Blackberries fruit on second-year floricanes; remove all fruited canes at the base after harvest and thin new primocanes to 4–6 per hill. Erect types can be tip-pruned at 3–4 ft in midsummer to encourage lateral branching and larger yields. Harvest when berries are fully black, slightly soft, and the white core is not visible — they do not ripen further off the plant. Trailing cultivars require a T-trellis; thornless varieties are easier to manage but may be less cold-hardy.

planting.note: Plant in early spring after frost danger passes; boysenberries are trailing types requiring a stout trellis.

directions: Boysenberries are vigorous trailing hybrids (blackberry x raspberry x loganberry lineage) that must be trained to a two- or three-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft. Cut all fruited canes to the ground after the summer harvest and tie new canes along the wires for next year's crop. Harvest when berries turn deep maroon-purple and have a rich, winey aroma. In zone 5–6 winters, remove canes from wires and lay them on the ground under mulch for protection.

planting.note: Plant one male plant for every 5–7 female plants in early spring; buffaloberry is wind-pollinated and both sexes must be within 50 ft.

directions: Silver buffaloberry is an extremely cold-hardy nitrogen-fixing shrub native to the Great Plains, valued for its bright red, tart berries that have historically been used by Indigenous peoples of North America for pemmican and sauces. It is dioecious and wind-pollinated, requiring male and female plants; purchase sexed plants, as seedlings cannot be sexed until flowering at 3–5 years. The thorny, silver-leaved plants thrive in alkaline, dry, or infertile soils where other fruits fail. Harvest by spreading a tarp and gently shaking or beating branches after a light frost, which softens the bitterness; berries are very tart raw but excellent cooked.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost in a sheltered site; appreciates afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Chilean guava (Ugni molinae) is a compact, evergreen myrtle-family shrub native to Chile, grown for its small, crimson, aromatic berries with a flavor often described as strawberry-vanilla-bubblegum. The Queen of England's personal favorite berry, it is widely grown in the UK and mild coastal climates. It tolerates some frost but is best suited to USDA zones 8–10 in the ground, or grown in containers and overwintered frost-free. Prune lightly after harvest to maintain shape; no heavy pruning is needed. Harvest in late fall and winter when berries are deep red-purple and fully aromatic.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in acidic, peaty, boggy or moist woodland soil; requires both male and female plants for fruit set.

directions: Cloudberry is a low-growing, rhizomatous Arctic-boreal species that requires cold winters, strongly acidic peat or sphagnum-rich soil (pH 3.5–5.0), and consistent moisture — it is exceptionally difficult to cultivate outside its native subarctic habitat. It is dioecious, so both male and female plants must be planted in proximity for fruit to set. The amber-golden berries are among the most highly prized wild fruits in Scandinavia and are used in traditional preserves; ripe berries are extremely soft and must be processed immediately. Not suited to continental climates south of USDA zone 5.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; highly adaptable to dry, alkaline soils unlike most Ribes.

directions: Clove currant (Ribes odoratum, syn. R. aureum) is a tough, drought-tolerant native prairie shrub with intensely clove-spice-scented yellow spring flowers and glossy black berries in mid-summer. It tolerates alkaline, clay, or droughty soils far better than European currants, making it the best Ribes choice for the Great Plains and intermountain West. The berries range from mild to somewhat astringent depending on selection; look for named fruit-producing cultivars such as 'Crandall'. Prune by removing the oldest canes at the base every few years; the plant suckers moderately and can form a thicket if not managed. Verify state restrictions before planting.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in acidic, peaty, moisture-retentive soil with access to irrigation.

directions: Cranberries require strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0), consistent moisture, and sandy-peat substrate — conditions that closely mimic their native bog habitat. A 3–4 in layer of coarse sand mulch helps suppress weeds and maintains the proper surface environment. The vines are slow to establish and typically take 3–4 years to produce a meaningful crop. Harvest in fall when berries turn deep red and bounce when dropped; home growers pick by hand rather than flooding.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; at least two cultivars within 60 ft improve cross-pollination and yields.

directions: Elderberries are fast-growing shrubs that fruit on current-season wood arising from two-year-old stems; cut one-third of the oldest canes to the ground each late winter to keep the planting vigorous. Plant two or more cultivars for cross-pollination — solo plantings produce far fewer berries. Harvest entire umbels (flat-topped clusters) in late summer when at least 95% of berries in a cluster are dark purple-black. Raw berries and green plant parts contain cyanogenic glycosides; always cook berries before eating.

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; primocane-fruiting types can yield a fall crop in their first year.

directions: Primocane-fruiting (everbearing) raspberries bear on the top portion of first-year canes in fall, then on lower portions the following summer if left. For the simplest management, mow all canes to the ground each late winter and enjoy a single fall crop on new primocanes — this eliminates disease buildup and minimizes pruning. Harvest from late summer through first frost when berries release easily from the plug. Consistent moisture during fruit development prevents crumbling.

planting.note: Plant after last frost in spring; requires a long frost-free season of 200–240 days for fruit to mature.

directions: Fuzzy kiwi (the standard grocery-store kiwifruit) requires a long, warm growing season and is best suited to zones 7–9 with at least 200 frost-free days. Like hardy kiwi, it is dioecious and requires male and female plants — one male pollinates up to 8 females. Train vines on a T-bar or pergola trellis and prune hard each dormant season to manage the vigorous growth. Harvest in late fall before hard frost when the seeds have turned black and the flesh is tender; fruit ripens off the vine.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed, spacing plants 24–30 in apart.

directions: Garden huckleberry (Solanum scabrum) is an African-origin annual grown for its jet-black, blueberry-sized fruit, which must be cooked before eating as raw berries contain solanine. Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 75–80°F and transplant outdoors after frost danger has passed. The bushy plants are prolific producers and generally need no staking. Harvest when all berries in a cluster have turned uniformly shiny black; cook with sugar and lemon juice to reduce bitterness and break down solanine before use in jams or pies.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring after frost danger; goji tolerates alkaline soils and drought once established.

directions: Goji berries are highly adaptable, tolerating poor, alkaline soils where other fruits fail, but perform best in well-drained loam with a pH of 6.5–8.0. The arching, slightly thorny canes can be left as a shrub or trained to a central leader with a stake. Prune lightly after fruiting to maintain shape and encourage new fruiting laterals. Harvest ripe berries by gently rolling the stem between your fingers — ripe fruits are bright red-orange, soft, and drop easily (avoid touching them, as the sap stains).

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; most golden raspberries are primocane-fruiting and will yield a fall crop in their first year.

directions: Golden raspberries are yellow-fruited mutations of red raspberry cultivars, most commonly primocane-fruiting (everbearing) types such as 'Anne' or 'Fall Gold', with exceptionally sweet, mild flavor and low acidity. Manage as you would a primocane-fruiting red raspberry: for the simplest system, mow all canes to the ground each late winter and allow new primocanes to grow and fruit in fall. For a two-crop system, retain canes over winter to produce a summer crop on floricanes. Harvest when berries turn fully golden-yellow and release easily; they are more fragile than red types and should be processed quickly.

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; fall planting works well in milder zones.

directions: Gooseberries tolerate partial shade better than most fruits and prefer cool, well-drained soil with a pH of 6.0–6.5. Prune to an open-vase structure each late winter, removing canes older than 3 years and any crossing branches to reduce disease pressure. Pick berries early for cooking (when green and firm) or allow them to ripen to red, yellow, or white for fresh eating. Note: check local regulations, as Ribes species are restricted in some U.S. states due to white pine blister rust.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; goumi is partially self-fertile but yields improve with cross-pollination from a second plant.

directions: Goumi is a nitrogen-fixing shrub from East Asia that produces abundant small, cherry-red, speckled fruits with a sweet-tart flavor reminiscent of cherry. It is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, making it a valued plant in permaculture systems. Prune lightly after fruiting to shape the plant and encourage denser branching; heavy pruning stimulates excessive vegetative growth at the expense of fruit. Harvest when berries are fully red and slightly soft — ripe fruits have little to no astringency and taste excellent fresh or dried.

planting.note: Transplant starts after all frost danger has passed; start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost.

directions: Ground cherries are grown as warm-season annuals in most climates; start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after danger of frost has passed. The bushy plants produce prolific small fruits enclosed in papery husks and often sprawl without support — a tomato cage or light staking helps. Harvest when the papery husk has dried to tan-brown and the golden berry inside detaches easily. Fallen fruits left in their husks on the ground remain fresh for several weeks.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger; provide a very sturdy trellis or pergola capable of holding hundreds of pounds of vine.

directions: Hardy kiwi vines are dioecious — plant one male vine for every 6–8 female vines or choose a self-fertile cultivar such as 'Issai'. The vines can take 4–7 years to bear fruit but become very productive once established, requiring a pergola or heavy-gauge trellis rated to hold at least 200 lbs. Prune to a permanent framework of main arms and renewal spurs each late winter, cutting lateral shoots back to 2–4 buds. Harvest when smooth, grape-sized fruits yield slightly to pressure and taste sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root or container stock in early spring once soil is workable; fall planting is possible in zones 6–8.

directions: Blueberries require acidic soil with a pH of 4.5–5.5; amend with elemental sulfur and use acidic mulch such as pine bark or wood chips 3–4 in deep. Plant at least two cultivars for cross-pollination and better fruit set. Prune out old canes older than 6 years at the base each late winter to stimulate productive new wood. Harvest when berries turn deep blue and detach with minimal resistance, typically 7–10 days after color change.

planting.note: Plant two or more compatible cultivars in early spring for cross-pollination; haskap blooms very early and needs a matching early-blooming partner.

directions: Honeyberries are among the earliest fruits to ripen — often 2–3 weeks before strawberries — and flowers can survive temperatures as low as -4°F, making them ideal for short-season climates. Two compatible cultivars from the same breeding group must be planted for cross-pollination. Minimal pruning is needed for the first 5 years; thereafter, remove a few of the oldest, thickest canes annually to renew. Harvest when berries are entirely blue-purple inside (cut one open to check), as color alone can be misleading.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; jostaberry is self-fertile and a single plant will produce fruit.

directions: Jostaberry is a thornless hybrid of black currant and gooseberry, combining the vigor and disease resistance of both parents with a flavor profile between the two. It is significantly larger and more vigorous than either parent, and is notably resistant to gooseberry mildew and currant big bud mite. Prune as you would a black currant — remove one-third of the oldest canes each late winter to stimulate new productive growth. Harvest in mid-summer when berries have turned from red to very dark purple-black and developed a sweet, slightly musky flavor; verify local Ribes restrictions before planting.

planting.note: Plant after last frost with a very sturdy pergola or trellis; self-fertile cultivars such as 'Issai' are suitable for single-plant planting.

directions: Kiwiberry is a compact-fruited selection of hardy kiwi (Actinidia arguta) grown specifically for smooth-skinned, grape-sized fruits that are eaten whole without peeling — they have the same flavor as standard kiwifruit but ripen 3–4 weeks earlier. Self-fertile cultivars such as 'Issai' and 'Ken's Red' allow single-plant production, but yields are higher when a male pollenizer is present. The extremely vigorous vines require a heavy-gauge trellis or pergola capable of bearing hundreds of pounds of vine and fruit; prune hard each dormant season to renewal spurs. Harvest when fruits yield to gentle pressure and taste sweet with no starchiness.

planting.note: Plant rooted plugs or divisions in early spring in acidic, well-drained soil; also useful as a groundcover under acid-loving shrubs.

directions: Lingonberries require acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) and perform well as an ornamental groundcover in zones 2–7. They produce two crops per year — a smaller crop in midsummer and a larger one in fall. Minimal pruning is needed; shear plants to 3–4 in every few years to rejuvenate. Harvest when berries are deep red and slightly tart; they store well refrigerated and are traditionally used in Scandinavian preserves and sauces.

planting.note: Plant rooted tip cuttings in early spring after last frost; provide a sturdy T-trellis at planting since canes reach 8–10 ft.

directions: Loganberry, a natural hybrid of blackberry and raspberry discovered in California in the 1880s, produces long, dark red, tart berries that are excellent for jams and wine. Train the vigorous trailing canes on a two- or three-wire trellis, weaving new primocanes along the wires during the growing season. After the summer harvest, cut all floricanes that bore fruit to the ground and secure the new primocanes in their place. The thornless 'LY59' strain is preferred for home gardens; in borderline zones, lay canes down and mulch after leaf drop.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in early spring in very acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Lowbush blueberries require strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0) and spread by underground rhizomes to form colonies. Manage in a two-year cycle: mow or burn half the planting to the ground each year so alternating sections produce a full crop annually. Harvest in midsummer when berries are deep blue-purple and sweet. Drought-tolerant once established, but irrigation during fruit fill improves berry size.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or tip-rooted canes in early spring as soon as soil is workable, with a T-trellis capable of handling 8-ft canes installed at the same time.

directions: Marionberry is a trailing blackberry hybrid (Chehalem x Olallie) developed by Oregon State University and is the most widely grown blackberry in the Pacific Northwest, prized for its deep, complex, slightly tart flavor. The thorny, vigorous canes must be trained along a two-wire trellis; weave new primocanes along the lower wire during the summer and tie them to the upper wire the following spring. After the mid-summer harvest, remove all fruited floricanes at the base and train the new primocanes into their place. Marionberries are sensitive to freezes below about 10°F; in colder areas, protect canes over winter.

planting.note: Plant grafted named cultivars in early spring in moist to wet, acidic, bottomland soil for best fruit production.

directions: Mayhaw is a small flowering hawthorn tree native to swampy bottomlands of the Gulf South, prized for its tart red fruits — ripening in May — that make exceptional jelly of deep pink color. It is well adapted to poorly drained, acidic, wet soil and is often planted along pond edges or low spots. Grafted cultivars such as 'Big Red', 'Super Berry', and 'Texas Star' bear much more reliably and earlier than seedlings. Fruit is harvested by spreading tarps under the tree and shaking, or by netting floating fruit from ponds in traditional southern production.

planting.note: Plant after last frost in full sun with a trellis or fence; roots are cold-hardy to zone 6 and re-sprout from the roots each spring.

directions: Maypop (Passiflora incarnata) is a North American native passionflower that is cold-hardy to zone 6, distinguishing it clearly from the tropical Passiflora edulis (passionfruit). The ornamental lavender flowers are stunning and the egg-sized yellow-green fruits have sweet, aromatic pulp similar to tropical passionfruit. Plants die back to the roots in zone 6–7 winters and re-sprout vigorously in late spring — do not remove the crown before new growth appears. Plant cross-pollinators nearby (more than one plant) for reliable fruit set. Harvest when fruits turn yellowish-green and fall or detach easily.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in early spring; use self-fertile cultivars or plant one male ('bronze') vine per 4–6 female vines.

directions: Muscadine grapes are native to the southeastern U.S. and far more heat- and disease-resistant than European or labrusca types. Train to a single trunk with two permanent lateral cordons on a high-wire trellis at 5–6 ft; spur-prune cordons each dormant season to 2–3 bud spurs spaced 4–6 in apart. Many cultivars are not self-fertile, so check pollination requirements before purchasing. Harvest by shaking or hand-picking individual berries when they reach full color and slip easily from the skin.

planting.note: Plant two or more plants for cross-pollination in early spring; extremely adaptable to cold, dry, or alkaline sites.

directions: Nanking cherry is one of the hardiest fruiting shrubs available, reliably surviving temperatures below -40°F and tolerating poor, dry, or alkaline soils. The abundant small red cherries ripen in early summer and have a sweet, slightly tart flavor excellent for jams, juices, and fresh eating. Two or more plants are needed for cross-pollination; plants flower very early in spring and may need frost protection of blossoms in zones 3–4 during cold snaps. Prune after harvest to remove dead wood and open the canopy; the shrub is generally very healthy with minimal pest and disease problems.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in spring in humus-rich, strongly acidic, moist woodland soil in full shade.

directions: Partridgeberry is a native North American groundcover for shaded, acidic woodland gardens, forming a dense mat of evergreen, trailing stems barely 2 in tall. The bright red, twin-fused berries persist through winter and are edible but bland and seedy, making them more valuable as wildlife food and ornamental ground cover than for human harvest. Each berry is the fused product of two twin flowers — visible as two distinct calyxes on mature fruit. Grow in strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) under deciduous trees; it is slow to establish but extremely long-lived with virtually no maintenance needed.

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed in zones 9–11; in cooler zones grow in large containers and overwinter indoors.

directions: Passionfruit is a vigorous, tendril-climbing vine requiring a sturdy wire trellis or fence. Train the main stem vertically and allow lateral shoots to drape horizontally from the top — these laterals bear the flowers and fruit. Prune heavily after fruiting to rejuvenate the vine and maintain productive growth. Harvest when the fruit skin begins to wrinkle and the fruit falls or detaches easily; purple cultivars are generally more cold-tolerant than yellow types.

planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns in early spring with crowns level at the soil surface; must be grown near standard red strawberries for adequate pollination.

directions: Pineberry is a white-fruited strawberry cultivar (largely derived from the South American species Fragaria chiloensis crossed with F. x ananassa) that produces pale cream-white berries with red achenes and a distinctive pineapple-like aroma when fully ripe. Pineberries have low self-fertility and require a red-fruiting strawberry variety planted nearby as a pollinator for adequate fruit set. Grow exactly as you would a june-bearing or everbearing strawberry, removing blossoms the first season to build root strength. Harvest when berries are white to pale pink with red seeds and give off a sweet pineapple fragrance.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring as soon as soil is workable.

directions: Red currants fruit heavily on spurs of wood two years old and older; prune each late winter to keep 8–12 canes of mixed ages and remove any cane older than 5 years. They tolerate more shade than most fruits and appreciate afternoon shelter in hot climates. Harvest entire strigs (clusters) when berries are uniformly bright red and slightly translucent. Check state restrictions on Ribes planting before ordering.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in moist, strongly acidic, well-drained soil under conifer canopy.

directions: Salal is an evergreen native shrub of the Pacific Northwest coast, found beneath conifers from central California to Alaska, where it forms dense thickets on acidic, well-drained forest soils. The dark blue-purple berries are similar in size to a blueberry but have mealy, mildly sweet flesh and were an important food source for coastal Indigenous peoples, eaten fresh or dried into cakes. Salal is propagated by division or from container-grown nursery stock; seed germination is slow and unreliable. The glossy foliage is commercially valuable in the floral trade, and home growers can cut leathery stems for arrangements without harming the plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich soil; salmonberry naturalizes readily in Pacific Northwest conditions.

directions: Salmonberry is a Pacific Northwest native shrub that thrives in moist to wet, shaded sites along streams and forest edges, making it one of the few fruiting canes suitable for boggy ground. Fruits ripen very early — often in May or June — in shades from yellow-orange to deep red-salmon, with a mild, slightly bland flavor best suited to fresh eating or jams. Thin old canes to the ground each year after fruiting to maintain vigor, as the plant spreads by rhizomes and can form dense thickets. Wildlife value is extremely high; berries attract birds and bears within days of ripening.

planting.note: Plant one male plant for every 5–7 female plants in early spring; sea buckthorn is wind-pollinated and needs close proximity between sexes.

directions: Sea buckthorn is a thorny, nitrogen-fixing shrub that thrives in poor, dry, sandy soils where most fruits struggle. It is dioecious and requires male plants for pollination — one male per 5–7 females; sex is not identifiable until first flowering, so purchase sexed plants. The tiny berries are borne directly on the branches in dense clusters and are difficult to harvest without damaging the plant; a common approach is to cut fruiting branches in fall and strip berries indoors or by freezing and shaking. The tart, orange berries are extremely high in vitamin C.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring while still dormant; fall planting works well in zones 5–8.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–7.0) in full to partial sun; serviceberry tolerates wet sites and woodland edges better than most fruiting shrubs. No cross-pollinator is required, though planting two or more selections increases yield. Fruits ripen in late spring to early June, turning from red to deep purple-blue when fully sweet — birds will compete for them, so netting is advisable. Prune crossing or crowded stems in late winter to maintain an open, multi-stemmed form and remove root suckers to control spread.

planting.note: Set crowns in early spring; everbearing types can yield a partial crop the first fall if planted promptly.

directions: Plant in spring with crowns at soil level; remove only the spring flush of blossoms and allow the summer and fall crops to set. Everbearing types produce two distinct flushes — early summer and late summer into fall — with fewer runners than june-bearing types, making them well suited to hill culture or raised beds. Harvest when berries are fully red and fragrant. Replace plants every 3–4 years as productivity declines.

planting.note: Set bare-root crowns in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, keeping the crown level with the soil surface.

directions: Plant bare-root crowns in spring with the crown at soil level, setting runners into a matted-row system 12–18 in apart. Pinch all blossoms the first year to build root vigor; harvest begins the following June when berries are uniformly red and pull easily from the stem. Renovate beds immediately after harvest by mowing foliage to 3–4 in and thinning plants to 4–6 per square foot. Mulch crowns with straw in fall in zones 4 and colder.

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root canes in early spring as soon as soil can be worked.

directions: Summer raspberries fruit on floricanes — canes produced in year one that overwinter and bear fruit the following summer. After harvest, cut all fruited floricanes to the ground and thin new primocanes to 4–6 per linear foot for best airflow. Support canes on a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft. Harvest when berries pull cleanly from the plug with no resistance, typically over 3–4 weeks in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root vines in early spring; install a sturdy trellis or arbor before planting.

directions: Table grapes require a robust trellis capable of supporting 15–30 lbs of fruit per vine; the two-wire Kniffin system or a pergola are common choices. Train a permanent trunk and 2–4 fruiting arms during the first 3 years; thereafter, annual dormant pruning should remove 90% of the previous year's growth, leaving spurs or canes with 2–4 buds each. Thin clusters after fruit set to improve berry size and airflow. Harvest when berries reach full color, the tendrils nearest the cluster have dried, and sugar is confirmed by taste.

planting.note: Plant rooted tip cuttings or bare-root canes in early spring and install a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft at the same time.

directions: Tayberry is a Scottish-bred hybrid (blackberry x raspberry) with long, conical, deep purple-red berries that ripen in mid-summer; the flavor is richer and sweeter than loganberry. Train the long trailing canes fan-style along wires, keeping current-year primocanes separate from fruiting floricanes to simplify post-harvest cleanup. After harvest, remove all fruited canes to the ground and tie in the new primocanes. In zone 5–6, protect canes over winter by detaching them from wires and covering with straw mulch or a frost-cloth tunnel.

planting.note: Plant root divisions or container-grown plants in early spring in partial shade with moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Thimbleberry is a large-leafed, thornless native Rubus that thrives along woodland edges and mountain slopes from Alaska to Mexico, tolerating more shade than almost any other fruiting cane. The soft, bright red berries resemble a wide, shallow thimble and have a delicate, sweet flavor but are extremely fragile — they must be eaten or processed within hours of harvest. Propagate by dividing root clumps in spring; seed germination is erratic and requires cold stratification. Allow the plant to naturalize in a semi-wild planting rather than managing it as a formal fruiting cane.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; white currants perform well trained as a cordon against a north- or east-facing wall.

directions: White currant is a yellow-fruited mutation of red currant sharing identical cultivation requirements: it fruits most heavily on spurs of two-year-old and older wood. The translucent, ivory-white berries are sweeter and milder than red currants and are prized for elegant fresh-eating and jellies. Prune each late winter to maintain 8–12 canes of mixed ages, removing canes older than 5 years. Harvest entire strigs when berries have turned fully translucent white-golden and taste sweet; check local Ribes restrictions before purchasing.

planting.note: Plant grafted or own-rooted dormant vines in early spring on a well-drained slope with good air drainage.

directions: Wine grapes thrive in lean, well-drained soils with a pH of 5.5–6.5 and perform best on slopes with excellent air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Establish a vertical shoot positioning (VSP) trellis with catch wires at 4 and 5 ft above the cordon wire. Rigorous dormant pruning — leaving only 30–60 buds per vine depending on vigor — is essential for fruit quality. Harvest timing is cultivar- and use-specific; measure Brix (sugar) with a refractometer and monitor seed color and berry flavor.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in early spring; note that wineberry is invasive in much of the eastern U.S., so check local regulations before planting.

directions: Wineberry, native to Japan and China, produces small, brilliant red, jewel-like drupes with a sweet, delicate flavor; it is grown for fruit production in Asia and has naturalized extensively in the eastern U.S. and should be grown only in contained settings or where legal. The canes are covered in red glandular hairs and tip-root readily, so remove spent canes promptly and clear any rooted tips. Harvest when berries are uniformly bright red and detach easily from the sticky calyx — they do not ripen after picking and must be used within 1–2 days. Pruning follows the same floricane/primocane cycle as standard raspberries.

planting.note: Sow in early spring around the last frost date, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before first frost to allow fall establishment.

directions: Inoculate seed with Medicago-specific rhizobium and broadcast at 15–20 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft), raking seed to 1/4 in depth into a firm, well-prepared seedbed with pH 6.5–7.0. Alfalfa fixes 100–200 lb of nitrogen per acre per year once established and deep taproots (up to 6 ft) break hardpan and mine subsoil minerals. Terminate by mowing at early bloom stage — typically every 30–40 days in summer — before the first killing frost; turning it under releases a large pulse of nitrogen. In perennial plantings, allow one cutting to go to seed to rejuvenate the stand every two or three years.

planting.note: Sow 2–6 weeks before first fall frost; germinates and establishes faster than perennial ryegrass and can be interseeded into standing late-season crops.

directions: Broadcast seed at 20–25 lb per acre (about 1/2 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a prepared surface and rake to 1/4 in depth; annual ryegrass has one of the fastest establishment rates of all cool-season grasses, providing rapid erosion control and weed suppression. The dense, fibrous root system scavenges residual nitrogen effectively, locking it up over winter to prevent leaching. Terminate in early spring before it joints and sets seed — once headed, it becomes very difficult to kill without herbicide or repeated tillage. Mow to 2–3 in and till, or use a roller-crimper at boot stage for no-till termination; allow residue 2–3 weeks to break down before planting.

planting.note: Sow in early fall 4–8 weeks before first frost, or in early spring; survives light frost but not hard freezes, so it winter-kills in zones 6 and colder.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 15–20 lb per acre, raking seed lightly to 1/4 in depth; berseem germinates in near-freezing soil and establishes faster than crimson clover. Unlike most clovers, berseem regrows vigorously after each mowing, allowing two to three cuts before termination, with each cut releasing a pulse of nitrogen. It tolerates moderate salinity and poorly drained soils better than most cool-season legumes, making it useful in difficult garden sites. Terminate by incorporating the last flush of growth 3–4 weeks before planting the next crop.

planting.note: Sow anytime in warm frost-free weather; a fast summer smother crop that flowers in ~5 weeks.

directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep into warm soil; it sprouts within days and shades out weeds quickly. Cut or turn it under at first bloom, before it sets seed, or it will reseed. It also draws pollinators and helps make soil phosphorus available.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; common vetch is slightly more cold-tolerant than crimson clover and grows in poorer soils than hairy vetch.

directions: Sow inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 20 sq ft) in fall, raking to 1/2 in depth; common vetch tolerates sandy, low-fertility soils where hairy vetch may struggle and establishes a climbing, vining habit that benefits from a grass companion for support. It fixes 80–120 lb of nitrogen per acre over winter and spring and is easier to terminate than hairy vetch because the vines are thinner and dry down faster. Mow at early bloom before pods swell to prevent reseeding; the narrow tendriled stems break down readily and release nitrogen within 2–3 weeks.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring; fixes nitrogen and blooms crimson.

directions: Broadcast seed onto a raked bed at about 1/4 in depth and keep moist until established. Let it grow through winter, then mow and turn it under a few weeks before planting to release its fixed nitrogen. Terminate at early bloom for the best nitrogen return.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost in fall, or in earliest spring; nitrogen-fixing and edible tender shoots.

directions: Sow inoculated seed about 1 in deep, often mixed with oats or rye for support. The vines fix nitrogen and produce edible pea shoots you can harvest before turning the crop under. Terminate at flowering for the best nitrogen contribution.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first frost, often mixed with winter rye which gives it something to climb.

directions: Sow seed about 1/2 in deep, ideally inoculated with the correct rhizobia and paired with a grass like rye for support. It overwinters and fixes a large amount of nitrogen by spring bloom. Terminate at early flowering for maximum nitrogen and easy breakdown.

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover-crop legumes.

directions: Broadcast or drill inoculated seed at 40–50 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 25 sq ft), 1/2 in deep in warm, well-drained soil; cowpeas germinate and grow rapidly, fixing 100–150 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing summer weeds with their spreading vines. The crop tolerates sandy, infertile, and slightly acidic soils where most legumes struggle. Terminate by mowing at early pod stage before seeds mature to prevent volunteer plants; large amounts of vine biomass decompose quickly in warm weather. Pairs well with sorghum-sudangrass for a combined legume-grass summer cover.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost; oats winter-kill below about 10°F, leaving a ready-to-plant mat of residue in spring without tillage.

directions: Broadcast seed at 50–80 lb per acre (about 1 oz per 30 sq ft) and rake to 1/2 in depth; oats establish quickly and produce substantial biomass before being killed by hard freezes. Their winter-killed residue lies flat as a weed-suppressing mulch that is easy to plant or transplant through in spring without additional termination work. Often mixed with field peas or hairy vetch to add nitrogen to the primarily carbonaceous grass residue. In mild-winter regions (zones 8–9), oats may overwinter and require mowing to terminate before spring planting.

planting.note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; the most heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season cover grass, thriving in Gulf Coast summers.

directions: Drill or broadcast seed 1/2 in deep into warm soil; pearl millet germinates and establishes faster than sorghum-sudangrass in sandy, low-fertility soils. It produces large amounts of biomass for smothering weeds and building organic matter, and tolerate short dry spells without wilting like corn or sorghum can. Mow or terminate before seed heads mature to prevent unwanted reseeding. Mix with cowpeas for a combined nitrogen-fixing and biomass cover.
sources: Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education (SARE) cover crop charts

planting.note: Plant rhizome sprigs in late spring once soil is warm; establishment is slow the first season but the stand fills in by year 2.

directions: Plant rhizome sections 2–3 in deep in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; perennial peanut tolerates poor, acidic soils typical of Gulf Coast landscapes. Establishment is slow the first year but the low, dense mat fixes nitrogen and crowds out weeds without mowing once established. It can be used as a lawn alternative, orchard under-planting, or living mulch in vegetable corridors. In zones 8–9 the top may freeze back in hard winters but the rhizomes resprout reliably from below ground.

planting.note: Sow 2–6 weeks before last spring frost, or in early fall in mild climates; flowers arrive in about 6 weeks and are among the best for attracting bees.

directions: Broadcast tiny seed at 3–5 lb per acre (a light dusting per 100 sq ft) on the surface of a firm, moist seedbed without covering, as seed needs light to germinate; rake in very lightly if birds are a problem. Phacelia establishes quickly in cool soil, outcompeting many weeds with dense, ferny foliage that also conserves soil moisture. The blue-purple flowers are exceptional for beneficial insects and bees — allow partial bloom before mowing to maximize pollinator benefit. Terminate by mowing just as the last flowers fade; it decomposes rapidly and leaves no allelopathic residue.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or frost-seed in late winter; short-lived perennial that fixes nitrogen and blooms its second year.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed onto a prepared bed at 8–10 lb per acre, raking to 1/4 in depth; red clover tolerates slightly acidic soils and moderate shade better than most legume cover crops. It overwinters in zones 4 and warmer and puts on vigorous growth in early spring, often topping 2 ft before bloom. Terminate at early flower stage — before the first florets open to brown — to capture maximum fixed nitrogen of 80–150 lb per acre. A second flush of growth often follows mowing, allowing a second cut of biomass before final incorporation.

scientific_name: Sorghum bicolor × Sorghum bicolor var. sudanense

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; produces massive biomass for weed suppression and soil building.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed 3/4 in deep into warm soil; sorghum-sudangrass hybrids germinate and grow aggressively, reaching 6–8 feet before termination. The dense canopy suppresses even aggressive perennial weeds, and allelopathic root exudates further inhibit weed germination. Mow to 4–6 in to encourage regrowth and additional tillering before final termination; allow residue 2–3 weeks to partially break down before transplanting into it. Avoid planting in soybean rotations immediately after, as the sorghum-family residue can briefly stunt legume establishment.
sources: Midwest Cover Crops Council field guide

planting.note: Sow after last frost once soil is consistently warm; a standout warm-season nitrogen fixer and nematode suppressor for zone 9–11.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed 1/2 in deep into warm, well-drained soil after danger of frost has passed. Sunn hemp grows rapidly — up to 6 feet in two months — and fixes 100–200 lb of nitrogen per acre while suppressing root-knot nematodes through root exudates. Terminate by mowing at first bloom (roughly 60–70 days) before seed set, since mature plants become woody and slow to decompose. Do not plant where livestock can graze, as the foliage contains alkaloids toxic to most grazing animals.

planting.note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; the deep taproot breaks up compacted soil and winter-kills in cold zones.

directions: Broadcast seed about 1/2 in deep in late summer; the thick taproot drills deep and shatters compaction. In cold-winter areas it dies and rots in place, leaving channels and released nutrients. Where it survives, mow before it flowers to prevent seeding.

planting.note: Frost-seed onto bare or thin ground in late winter to early spring; the freeze-thaw cycle works seed into the soil surface naturally.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 1/4–1/2 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a lightly raked surface; the tiny seed needs good soil contact but should not be covered more than 1/4 in. White clover spreads by stolons to form a dense living mulch that fixes nitrogen and feeds beneficial insects with nectar-rich flowers. Mow periodically to prevent flowering from shading out adjacent crops, or allow it to bloom freely when used as a pollinator corridor. In annual vegetable beds, turn the stand under in early spring and wait 2–3 weeks before planting so the nitrogen flush is available to the next crop.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; a fast-growing brassica smother crop with biofumigant properties.

directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb per acre (about 1/4 oz per 100 sq ft) onto a firm seedbed, raking to 1/4 in depth; mustard germinates in cold soil within days and establishes an aggressive canopy that smothers weeds. When chopped and incorporated at early flower stage, the glucosinolates in the tissue break down into biofumigant compounds that suppress soil-borne pathogens and weed seeds — a technique called biofumigation. Mow and immediately till to a 4–6 in depth, then water and tarp the bed for 2 weeks to trap fumigant gases. Terminate before it sets seed, as mustard reseeds abundantly and can become a weed.

planting.note: Sow from 6 weeks before to just after first frost; the most cold-hardy cover crop, germinating in near-freezing soil.

directions: Broadcast seed at about 1/2 in depth in fall; it germinates in cold soil when little else will. It scavenges leftover nitrogen and smothers weeds over winter, then puts on fast spring growth. Mow and turn under 2–3 weeks before planting, as its residue briefly suppresses small seeds.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and should be potted on before becoming root-bound. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil as soon as the risk of hard frost has passed; osteospermum thrives in cool weather and slows during peak summer heat. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage continuous bloom and pinch stem tips to promote branching early in the season. In zones 9–11 it may survive as a short-lived perennial; take stem cuttings in late summer to perpetuate favorite selections.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after nights stay reliably above 50°F.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in a warm starting mix and keep evenly moist until germination. Transplant after all frost risk is gone, spacing larger varieties 12–18 in apart. Deadhead large pompom blooms as they fade to redirect energy to new buds. Tall varieties may need staking in exposed, windy sites.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist starting mix without covering — light is essential for germination. Ageratum produces fluffy, powder-puff blooms that attract butterflies and beneficial insects. Shear plants back lightly when flowering slows or plants become leggy to promote a fresh flush of bloom. Consistent moisture is important, as dry conditions cause the plants to stop flowering.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Start seeds on the surface of a moist, fine-textured germination mix at 70–75°F; seeds need light to germinate so do not cover. Transplant into full sun after last frost, spacing 12–18 in apart in well-drained soil enriched with compost. Angelonia is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and rarely needs deadheading, as spent florets drop cleanly. Pinch stem tips early to encourage branching and a fuller plant shape.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; transplant is also possible starting 6–8 weeks early indoors.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep and cover lightly, as darkness can aid germination. Annual phlox prefers cool growing conditions and puts on its best show in spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters regularly to prevent seed set and keep flowers coming. In hot climates, plants may slow in midsummer heat; cut back and they will often rebloom as temperatures cool in fall.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has completely passed.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix, barely pressing in, as light aids germination. Grow on under bright light to prevent tall, weak seedlings before transplanting. Remove spent spikes at their base to encourage a continuous succession of new flower stems. Annual salvia blooms from early summer until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; refrigerate seeds for 5–7 days before sowing to improve germination rates. Transplant after the last frost date into well-drained soil, as verbena is very susceptible to root rot in wet conditions. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy to stimulate a fresh flush of bloom. Verbena thrives in heat and is drought tolerant once established.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only when nights are consistently above 55°F.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm, moist starting mix and maintain soil temperature above 75°F for best germination. Annual vinca is self-cleaning and requires no deadheading, shedding spent blooms on its own. It excels in summer heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant. Avoid planting in cold or wet soil, as chilling causes the foliage to yellow and growth to stall.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring as soon as soil is workable, or in fall for early establishment.

directions: Plant bulbs scales-down at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained, humus-rich soil. Lilies prefer cool roots but sunny tops; mulch the soil surface and underplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. Remove only spent flowers, never the stem and leaves, so the plant can photosynthesize and recharge the bulb. Red lily beetle is a serious pest in many regions; inspect plants regularly and remove beetles and larvae by hand.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in moist, organically rich soil with adequate moisture.

directions: Plant crowns so the top of the rhizome sits 1–2 in below the soil surface in consistently moist, humus-rich soil — astilbe does not tolerate drought and will brown and decline quickly without adequate moisture. The feathery plumes in shades from white through pink to deep red are spectacular in shade and semi-shade gardens from late spring through summer. Leave the russet dried plumes standing through fall and winter for ornamental interest. Divide every 3–4 years in spring when flowering declines or the crown heaves above the soil surface.

planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; seeds germinate in cool soil and young plants are frost hardy. For fall bloom, sow again in late summer.

directions: Direct sow seed 1/4 in deep in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked. Bachelor buttons resent transplanting due to a taproot and grow best when started in place. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong blooming, or allow a few to set seed for natural reseeding. Plants decline in summer heat; a second sowing in late summer yields a flush of fall bloom in cool climates.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow outdoors in early spring; blooms appear the second year from seed.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just barely covering them, since light aids germination; thin or transplant to 12–18 in spacing. Balloon flowers are late to emerge in spring — mark their location to avoid accidentally digging the fleshy taproot. Deadhead spent flowers to extend the blooming period through summer, and avoid dividing since the taproot resents disturbance. Plants are long-lived and improve in beauty over many years with minimal care.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in late summer to early fall so roots establish before frost; divisions planted after midsummer bloom by the following spring.

directions: Set rhizomes horizontally with the top surface exposed to the sun — never bury them, as iris borers and rot thrive under covered rhizomes. Plant in late summer after bloom, with the fan of leaves pointing in the direction you want growth to spread. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become overcrowded, discarding the woody center and replanting healthy outer fans. Good drainage is essential; soggy soil causes rhizome rot.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil with good air circulation.

directions: Plant in moist but well-drained soil rich in organic matter; good air circulation around plants is essential to minimize powdery mildew, their primary disease issue. The shaggy, two-lipped flowers are magnets for hummingbirds, bees, and butterflies from midsummer into fall. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering and cut plants back by one-third after the first flush to encourage a second wave. Divide every 2–3 years in spring because the spreading rhizomes exhaust the soil in the clump's center.

planting.note: Direct sow on or near last frost date; seeds need light to germinate so press onto soil surface.

directions: Press seeds onto the soil surface — they need light to germinate and should not be covered. Plants often bloom their first year from an early start, flowering freely into fall. Deadhead to prolong blooming or allow seeds to self-sow for a naturalized planting. Rudbeckia fulgida varieties are longer-lived perennials; divide every 3 years to refresh the planting.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; do not cover seeds, as light aids germination.

directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist growing medium and provide warmth (70–75°F) and bright light without covering; transplant after frost into sharply drained, even poor soil since gaillardia thrives on neglect. Deadhead spent blooms continuously to keep the bold red and yellow flowers coming from early summer through frost. Avoid clay soils and overwatering — wet conditions in winter are the primary cause of plant loss. Divide or replace plants every 2–3 years, as they tend to be short-lived perennials.

planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked; dormant division in fall is also successful.

directions: Plant crowns 1–2 in deep in cool, humus-rich, consistently moist but well-drained soil in partial shade. Bleeding heart goes dormant by midsummer in warm climates — interplant with hostas, ferns, or other shade-lovers to fill the gap left when the foliage yellows and dies back. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring just as new growth emerges; the fleshy roots are brittle, so handle with care. All parts of the plant are mildly toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Direct sow in very early spring or fall; seeds germinate in cool soil and seedlings tolerate frost.

directions: Surface sow by scattering seed on bare ground and pressing lightly without covering, as light aids germination. Breadseed poppies resent root disturbance and must be sown in place. Thin to 6–12 in when seedlings are 2 in tall. The ornamental seedpods are striking after petals drop and are valuable dried; harvest when the cap at the top turns from green to tan.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.

directions: Press seeds onto moist soil surface or cover very lightly; they need light to germinate and germinate readily in 7–14 days. Unlike Rudbeckia hirta, R. triloba is a true biennial-to-short-lived-perennial that produces abundant small golden-yellow flowers with a dark center from midsummer through hard frost. Plant in full sun to partial shade in average garden soil; plants self-sow prolifically and naturalize easily in meadow and prairie plantings. Deadhead some stems to extend the season and let others set seed to perpetuate the colony.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in the refrigerator, then start indoors; or direct sow in fall for natural stratification and spring emergence.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds by mixing with moist sand in a sealed bag and refrigerating for 30 days before sowing at soil surface — seeds need light and should not be covered. Butterfly weed has a deep taproot that makes it drought tolerant once established but very sensitive to transplanting; direct sowing or using deep individual cells causes the least disturbance. First-year plants rarely bloom, investing energy into root development; blooms appear reliably from the second year onward in vivid orange clusters that are among the most attractive nectar sources for monarchs and other butterflies. Leave seedheads through winter to self-sow and feed birds.

planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost in cool soil; young plants tolerate frost and prefer cool weather.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in cool, well-drained soil in early spring. Calendula prefers cool weather and will slow or stop blooming when summer heat arrives, often rebounding in fall. Deadhead consistently to prevent seed set and extend the blooming period. The ray petals are edible and can be used as a garnish or in salads.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil to germinate and tolerate light frost.

directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and rake lightly to cover 1/16–1/8 in deep. California poppies hate transplanting due to a delicate taproot, so sow where plants are to grow. They thrive in poor, dry soils and need little or no supplemental water once established. Deadhead to extend bloom, or let plants self-sow freely for a naturalized planting.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after the last frost when soil is warm; or start indoors in pots 4–6 weeks early.

directions: Plant rhizomes 2–4 in deep with the growing eyes pointing up after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed; starting in pots indoors 4–6 weeks early extends the season in short-summer regions. Cannas are heavy feeders — apply a balanced slow-release fertilizer at planting and a monthly liquid feed during the growing season. Deadhead by removing the entire spent flower spike back to a side shoot to keep the display going. In zones below 8, dig rhizomes after frost, allow to dry for a day, then pack in barely moist peat or newspaper at 50–55°F for winter storage.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; surface-sow without covering as seeds need light to germinate.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix and provide light at 65–70°F; seedlings emerge in 2–3 weeks and grow slowly their first season. Plant in consistently moist to wet soil — rain gardens, pond edges, and low-lying areas suit this plant perfectly. Brilliant red spikes appear in midsummer to early fall and are hummingbird magnets. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years), so allow self-sowing or divide offsets each spring to maintain the colony.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil; plants establish quickly and bloom the first season.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained soil — catmint thrives in conditions that would challenge many perennials and blooms heavily from spring into early summer. Shear the entire plant back by one-half after the first flowering flush to refresh the mounding habit and trigger a second wave of lavender-blue flowers in late summer. The silvery-green aromatic foliage is deer-resistant and provides a reliable edging throughout the season. Divide in spring every 3–4 years or propagate from cuttings taken in late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant only after soil is warm, as cold temperatures cause stunted plants.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and lightly press in — seed needs light and warmth to germinate. Do not rush transplanting into cold soil, as chilling causes plants to stall and go prematurely to seed. Deadhead spent plumes on crested and feathered types to extend the display. Celosia makes an excellent dried flower; harvest spikes just as they reach full color.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix at 65–70°F. Rotate planting locations each year, as China asters are prone to Fusarium wilt when grown in the same soil repeatedly. Avoid overhead watering and ensure good air circulation to reduce disease pressure. Harvest cut flowers when petals are just beginning to open fully for maximum vase life.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost, as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and can be transplanted while still small. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral-to-slightly-alkaline soil; heavy clay and overwet conditions lead to crown rot. Deadhead spent flowers continuously to prevent early seed set and extend the blooming season well into summer. Plants often decline in intense summer heat but may rebloom in fall if sheared back and kept watered.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost while soil is still cool; succession sow every 3 weeks for extended bloom.

directions: Direct sow on the soil surface or barely cover; seeds germinate best at cool soil temperatures and seedlings dislike transplanting. Thin ruthlessly to 9–12 in because overcrowded plants bloom poorly and are prone to powdery mildew in humid conditions. Clarkia thrives on neglect in poor, well-drained soil — fertilizing too heavily causes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants naturally set seed and die when summer heat arrives; in mild-winter climates, sow in fall for late-winter to spring bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow at or 2 weeks before last frost; cold stratification of seed for 7 days in the refrigerator improves germination.

directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface with barely 1/8 in of cover — light aids germination. Cleome self-sows prolifically and can become weedy; deadhead to limit reseeding if preferred. The tall, airy flower heads open from the base upward over several weeks, providing an extended season of bloom. Handle plants with care as stems bear small spines and the foliage has a musky scent.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds indoors for 3–4 weeks before spring sowing.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly on the soil surface and provide 3–4 weeks of cold stratification (fall sowing outdoors achieves this naturally) to break dormancy. Columbines bloom most freely in their second and third years and are prolific self-seeders that will hybridize freely, creating a constantly shifting mix of colors. Leafminer damage creates white tunnels in the foliage but rarely threatens plant health; remove affected leaves and dispose of them. Plants are short-lived perennials (3–5 years) but the colony self-perpetuates readily through self-seeding.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds tolerate light frost and germinate best in cool soil.

directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and tamp lightly; good drainage is essential since coreopsis rots in wet soil over winter. Deadhead regularly to keep the bright yellow flowers coming all summer. Shear plants back by half after the first flush to encourage compact regrowth and a second bloom. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring to prevent the center from dying out.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; cosmos thrives in poor soil and needs no fertilizing.

directions: Press seed lightly onto the soil surface and cover with 1/4 in of fine soil. Cosmos germinates best in poor, well-drained soil — avoid fertilizing, which promotes foliage over flowers. Pinch young plants at 12 in to encourage branching. Deadhead regularly or allow seed to set for self-sowing the following year.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall, 6–10 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-winter to early-spring bloom.

directions: Plant corms pointed side up at a depth of 3–4 in in well-drained soil; they naturalize quickly and look best planted in large, informal drifts. Squirrels frequently dig freshly planted corms, so covering the bed with wire mesh until the ground freezes can protect plantings. Foliage is narrow and grassy; allow it to die back completely before removing. Species crocus tend to be more reliably perennial than large-flowered Dutch types.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times the bulb's height (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up. Daffodils naturalize readily and multiply over time; deer and rodents avoid them because the bulbs are mildly toxic. Allow foliage to yellow and die back naturally — at least 6 weeks — so the bulb can store energy for next year's bloom. Divide congested clumps every 5–7 years when flowering diminishes.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 60°F.

directions: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 in deep with the eye pointing up after the last frost when soil reaches 60°F; set a stake at planting time to avoid spearing the tuber later. Pinch the growing tip when plants are 12 in tall to encourage branching and more flowers. In zones below 8, dig tubers after the first killing frost, cure them for a week, then store in slightly damp peat at 40–50°F through winter. Disbudding side buds around the main bud produces exhibition-quality blooms.

planting.note: Plant bare-root divisions in spring or fall; set crowns just 1 in below soil level.

directions: Plant fans with the crown about 1 in deep, spreading roots outward over a small mound of soil in the hole. Water thoroughly after planting and keep consistently moist the first season. Divide overcrowded clumps every 4–5 years in early spring or late summer when bloom quality declines. Removing spent scapes after all buds have opened keeps the planting tidy.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; fresh seed germinates best and benefits from 1–2 weeks of cold stratification.

directions: Use fresh seed — viability drops quickly in storage — and cold-stratify for 1–2 weeks before sowing at 60–70°F for best germination. Transplant after the last frost into rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil and stake early, as the tall spikes are highly susceptible to wind damage. Cut flower spikes back to basal foliage after the first bloom to encourage a second, smaller flush in late summer. All parts of the plant are toxic; all plant contact should be followed by hand washing.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost risk passes.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist germination mix and provide light at 65–75°F; germination is slow and uneven over 10–14 days. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; excellent salt and drought tolerance makes this ideal for coastal and hot dry sites. The silvery white, felted foliage is the ornamental feature — pinch off yellow flower buds when they appear to keep plants neat and maintain vigorous leaf growth. In zones 8–11 dusty miller overwinters as a short-lived perennial.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; lobelia has extremely tiny seeds and a slow early growth rate.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-like seeds on moist fine-textured mix; do not cover, as they need light to germinate, and use a spray mist to avoid washing them away. Grow seedlings on at cool temperatures (60–65°F) once germinated; pot up in small clusters of 3–5 seedlings rather than separating individuals. Lobelia blooms best in cool weather and partial shade in hot climates, often slowing or stopping in midsummer heat — shear back by half and water regularly for rebloom as fall approaches. The vivid blue, trailing types are classic basket and container plants.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or scarify and cold-stratify seeds before spring planting.

directions: Scarify seeds and cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks before spring sowing, or direct sow in fall to let natural winter conditions break dormancy. Baptisia is a slow-starting plant — expect minimal bloom for the first 2–3 years, but once established, the clump requires virtually no care and lives for decades. The indigo-blue lupine-like flower spikes appear in late spring and are followed by attractive, inflated seedpods that rattle in the wind. Do not attempt to transplant established plants, as the deep taproot does not tolerate disturbance.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden or start indoors; unlike true perennial rudbeckia, heliopsis often blooms robustly in its first season from an early start. Plant in full sun in average, well-drained soil; plants tolerate heat and short dry periods better than helenium. Deadhead regularly throughout summer to keep the golden-yellow, semi-double or double flowers coming continuously from July through September. Divide clumps every 3 years in spring or fall to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost; firebush is a Florida and Gulf Coast native that quickly reaches shrub size and blooms nonstop in summer heat.

directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun; firebush tolerates heat, drought, and poor soils once established and reblooms continuously from late spring through frost. The tubular orange-red flowers are magnets for hummingbirds and butterflies, while the small black fruits that follow attract songbirds — making it one of the most wildlife-productive native shrubs for Gulf Coast gardens. In zone 8–9 the top freezes back in winter, but the plant regrows vigorously from the base each spring; cut dead stems to the ground once new growth begins. Native firebush (var. patens) is superior to the commonly sold 'Compacta' cultivar for wildlife value and cold hardiness.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Surface sow tiny seed on moist starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed is extremely small and needs light to germinate. Nicotiana tolerates partial shade better than most annuals and performs well in dappled light. Fragrant varieties release scent primarily in the evening, making them ideal planted near a patio or window. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to early fall so plants overwinter as rosettes and bloom in spring alongside tulips and daffodils.

directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface or barely cover them; they germinate best in cool conditions and seedlings overwinter as low rosettes in mild climates. Forget-me-nots self-sow prolifically once established — let plants go to seed, then pull them after flowering to control spread. The sky-blue flowers are traditionally combined with spring bulbs in beds and create a charming filler under shrubs in partial shade. Remove spent plants when temperatures rise, as they become straggly and die back in summer heat.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; can also start indoors 4–6 weeks early.

directions: Sow seed 1/4–1/2 in deep in a warm, sunny location after the last frost. Four o'clocks form a tuberous root and tolerate drought well once established. Flowers open in late afternoon and remain open through the following morning, releasing a sweet fragrance that attracts sphinx moths. Plants self-sow prolifically and the fleshy roots can be dug and stored in frost-prone areas.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds need light and should not be covered.

directions: Press seeds onto the surface of moist potting mix under bright light — do not cover, as light aids germination. Foxgloves are biennial or short-lived perennials; most produce a rosette in year 1 and the spectacular tall spikes in year 2 before setting seed and dying. Cutting the main spike after bloom often stimulates smaller side spikes for additional flowers, and self-sowing keeps the planting going indefinitely. All parts of the plant are highly toxic; use caution and wash hands thoroughly after handling.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix. Pinch out the first flower bud to encourage bushier branching and heavier bloom set. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to keep plants producing through summer. Blooms appear approximately 6–8 weeks after transplant and continue until frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring, 2–6 weeks after the last frost, for fall bloom.

directions: Plant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil; pinch growing tips every 2–3 weeks from planting until midsummer (around July 4) to develop compact, well-branched plants with dozens of buds. Mums are short-day plants that begin flower bud formation as nights lengthen; stop pinching by midsummer to avoid disrupting this process. Mulch heavily after the first hard frost to improve winter hardiness in colder zones. Divide annually in spring to rejuvenate the planting and maintain plant vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in early fall for establishment before frost.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil with good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew, which is the plant's main problem. Thin stems to 5–7 per clump in spring to improve airflow and produce larger flower trusses on the remaining stems. Deadhead the central flower cluster as it fades to encourage lateral buds to open and extend the bloom season. Divide every 3 years in spring, replanting only the outermost, most vigorous sections.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in loose, well-drained germination mix; plants develop a deep taproot early so pot on before roots become crowded. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil — gaura thrives on neglect and declines in fertile, wet conditions. The wand-like stems bearing butterfly-shaped white or pink flowers sway gracefully all season without deadheading. Cut plants back to 6 in after the first heavy bloom flush to encourage a tidy second flush through autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; they germinate readily at 65–75°F and grow quickly into compact transplants. Plant in sharply drained, even poor soil in the hottest, sunniest location available — gazania excels in sandy or gravelly beds where other plants fail.
