Flowers close at night and on overcast days, but modern hybrid varieties stay open longer than older types. Drought tolerance is excellent once established; overwatering and heavy clay soils are the most common causes of failure.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds vertically, pointed end down, barely covered, as they are sensitive to orientation; maintain 70–75°F for germination. Plant so the crown sits slightly above soil level to prevent crown rot, which is the most common cause of plant failure. Water at the base rather than overhead; gerberas prefer a dry-wet-dry cycle rather than constantly moist soil. Deadhead spent flowers by cutting the stem to the base to encourage continuous bloom from spring through fall in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant corms after the last frost; make successive plantings every 2 weeks through early summer for extended bloom.

directions: Plant corms 4–6 in deep in well-drained soil with the pointed end up after the last frost; succession plant every 2 weeks through midsummer for continuous cut flowers. Tall varieties need staking; a single bamboo cane tied loosely to the stem works well. In zones below 7, dig corms after frost blackens the foliage, dry for 2–3 weeks, remove offsets (cormels), and store at 35–45°F in mesh bags through winter. Harvest cut spikes when the lowest 2–3 buds show color for longest vase life.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants typically bloom in their second year from seed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; cold stratification is not required but may speed germination. Established globe thistle is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives in poor, sandy, or chalky well-drained soil — fertile, moist soil causes lax growth and flopping. The spiky steel-blue globe flowers are excellent for fresh cutting and dry perfectly for everlasting arrangements if cut just before fully open. Divide clumps in spring every 4–5 years using a sharp spade; they are slow to re-establish so divisions should be generous.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; soak seed overnight to speed germination. Gomphrena thrives in heat and humidity and is highly drought tolerant once established. The clover-like flowers hold their color and shape exceptionally well when dried — harvest stems just as the heads reach full color. Deadheading encourages continuous fresh bloom throughout the summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; tolerates a wide range of soil conditions once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a location with morning sun and afternoon shade; hardy geraniums are adaptable to poor soils and are generally low-maintenance once established. Shear plants back by one-half after the initial bloom to refresh the foliage and encourage a second flush of flowers. The attractive lobed leaves often turn red or orange in fall, extending the ornamental season. Divide every 3–5 years in spring to reinvigorate the planting.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after nicking the seed coat and soaking 24 hours; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Nick or file seed coats and soak in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination rates significantly. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil — hardy hibiscus is native to wetlands and performs best with consistent moisture but tolerates average garden beds with regular irrigation. This is a late riser in spring; the shoots emerge much later than other perennials, so mark plants to avoid accidental disturbance. The dinner-plate-sized flowers in white, pink, and crimson open each morning and close by evening, with new blooms produced daily from midsummer through frost.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; germination is slow (14–28 days) and benefits from bottom heat at 70–80°F consistently. Transplant into rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; heat brings out the intensely vanilla-scented, deep purple flower clusters. Pinch tips once or twice before transplanting to produce bushier plants with more flowering heads. In zones 10–11 heliotrope is a shrubby perennial; elsewhere take cuttings in late summer to overwinter prized fragrant selections indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in humus-rich, well-drained shade; transplanted divisions establish more reliably than seed.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in well-drained, humus-rich soil in dappled to full shade — morning sun is tolerated, but afternoon sun causes leaf scorch. Hellebores bloom in late winter to early spring, often through snow, and the nodding flowers face downward; tipping the pot or planting on a slope allows a better view of the flower interior. Remove old foliage in late winter before new growth emerges to improve air circulation and reduce leaf spot diseases. Hellebores self-sow freely, and seedlings from hybrid plants will vary; divide named selections in early spring to maintain specific colors.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring around the last frost date or in late summer for bloom the following year.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; hollyhocks are biennial or short-lived perennials that flower the second year from seed and self-sow freely to maintain the planting. Rust fungus is the most common disease — look for orange pustules on leaf undersides and remove affected foliage promptly, avoiding overhead watering. Plant against walls or fences and provide loose staking for the tall, top-heavy flowering spikes. Allow some seed to mature and fall to keep the colony self-renewing.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth emerges or in fall at least 6 weeks before frost.

directions: Set crowns at soil level in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; avoid planting too deep or crowns may rot. Hostas tolerate deep shade but produce their best foliage color with 2–4 hours of dappled morning light. Divide large clumps every 4–5 years in early spring by slicing through the crown with a sharp spade. Slugs are the primary pest; use iron phosphate bait around vulnerable new growth.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before hard frost, for intensely fragrant spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs at a depth of 6–8 in (about 3 times the bulb height) in well-drained soil with the pointed end up; wear gloves as the bulb sap can irritate sensitive skin. Hyacinths produce their most spectacular, dense spikes in year 1; subsequent years yield looser, more relaxed flower clusters that many gardeners find charming. Allow foliage to yellow completely before removing it so the bulb can replenish its energy. In warm climates (zones 8+), lift and chill bulbs for 6 weeks before replanting.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed into sharply drained soil.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, gritty mix without covering, as they need light to germinate; avoid overwatering at all stages. Plant in the hottest, driest, sunniest spot in the garden — sandy, rocky, or gravelly soil is ideal, and root rot is the main cause of failure in clay or poorly drained sites. The magenta to purple daisy-like flowers cover the succulent mats nearly all summer and reopen only in bright sunlight. Hardy ice plant spreads steadily to make a weed-suppressing groundcover on slopes and rock gardens.

planting.note: In zones 7–10 sow in fall 4–6 weeks before first frost for winter and spring bloom; in colder zones direct sow in very early spring as soon as soil can be worked.

directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seeds on well-prepared, fine-textured soil; seeds need light and cool temperatures (50–60°F) to germinate — warm soil prevents germination. Plants grow as basal rosettes through winter in mild climates before sending up slender stems topped with large, tissue-paper flowers in spring. Iceland poppies are cool-season plants that stop flowering when summer heat arrives; in cold climates treat them as an early-spring annual with fall-sown or indoor-started transplants set out very early. Harvest cut flowers when buds are just beginning to crack open color and sear stem ends in boiling water to maximize vase life.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed and nights are warm.

directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix without covering — light is required for germination. Impatiens are one of the most reliable shade-tolerant annuals and will self-clean most spent flowers. Pinch plants back once after transplanting to encourage compact, bushy growth. Water consistently; wilting in heat is common and plants recover overnight, but chronic drought reduces flowering.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a moist medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors; germination is improved but still irregular. Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; this native prairie plant tolerates occasional wet feet and dry spells once established. Cutting stems back by half in June (Chelsea chop) reduces ultimate height and prevents the need for staking without sacrificing the domed, mauve-pink flower clusters beloved by butterflies. Seedheads are ornamentally interesting into winter and provide food for goldfinches.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; most gardeners use purchased transplants due to slow, uneven germination.

directions: Sow seeds after lightly scarifying or nicking the seed coat; soak overnight to improve germination, which is slow and can take 4–6 weeks at 70–80°F. Transplant in full sun after all frost risk has passed; lantana thrives in heat, drought, and poor soil and blooms most freely under mild stress. The multicolored flower clusters change color as they age, giving plants a jewel-like effect; deadhead clusters as they fade to keep new flowers coming. Perennial in zones 9–11 where it can become invasive; grow as an annual elsewhere.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 4–8 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall in mild climates for early spring bloom. Seeds need cold stratification and prefer cool soil to germinate.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep directly in the garden as early as the ground can be worked — larkspur germinates best in cool soil below 65°F and may fail to sprout if the weather turns warm. Do not start indoors, as plants have taproots that do not transplant well. Thin to 9–12 in to promote sturdy stems. Allow some seed to fall for reliable self-sowing in subsequent years.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in spring as soon as the soil can be worked; they establish quickly and bloom the first season.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep with the flat side down in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; rich or wet soil leads to floppy growth. Liatris is unique in opening its flowers from the top of the spike downward, prolonging the bloom period for 3–4 weeks in midsummer. The purple to rose-pink spikes are outstanding cut flowers and highly attractive to butterflies and bees; stems last 10–14 days in a vase. Clumps multiply steadily; divide every 3–4 years in spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain strong bloom.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost date; lisianthus requires a very long lead time and is challenging from seed.

directions: Surface-sow tiny, pelleted seeds on sterile, fine-textured mix; do not cover as seeds need light and maintain steady 70–75°F. Seedlings grow extremely slowly for the first 8–10 weeks, so patience is essential — do not overwater at this stage as damping off is common. Transplant carefully after last frost into rich, well-drained, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun. The ruffled, rose-like blooms in white, purple, pink, and bicolor make lisianthus one of the finest cut flowers; harvest stems when 2–3 buds per stem have opened.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; do not start too early indoors as plants grow quickly and become rootbound.

directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and press lightly — seed needs light to germinate. Direct sowing is preferred as plants grow fast and resent transplanting. Thin seedlings to 18–24 in to allow the dramatic, rope-like crimson tassels to develop fully. The hanging panicles can be cut and hung upside down to dry when they reach full length.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in early spring; also sow in fall in zones 7–10 for spring bloom.

directions: Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil and rake in 1/8 in deep; nigella resents transplanting and germinates best in cool soil. Thin seedlings to 9–12 in to prevent overcrowding — plants are quick to grow and will self-sow freely if allowed to set the inflated seed pods. The blue or white flowers are surrounded by thread-like bracts that give the plant its misty appearance; after bloom the ornamental balloon-like pods are equally attractive and excellent for dried arrangements. Make succession sowings every 3–4 weeks until early summer for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow outdoors in early spring or fall; nick or soak seeds overnight to improve germination.

directions: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or soak overnight in water to soften the hard seed coat before sowing 1/4 in deep in cool, slightly acidic, well-drained soil. Lupines fix atmospheric nitrogen via root nodules and thrive in lean soils without heavy fertilization — too much nitrogen promotes foliage over flowers. They prefer cool summers; in hot climates, they often perform as cool-season annuals, blooming in late spring and fading by midsummer. Deadhead promptly after the first flowering spike fades to encourage a second flush and prevent excessive self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost; Mexican bush sage blooms in fall and is the showiest late-season salvia for zone 8–11 landscapes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Mexican bush sage is drought tolerant once established and demands good drainage over fertility. It is a short-day plant that blooms from September through frost, producing velvety purple flower spikes loved by hummingbirds and bumblebees; the later into fall it blooms, the more spectacular the display. Cut the entire plant back to 6–8 in after the last hard freeze in late winter to stimulate dense new basal growth. Propagate easily by dividing the clump in spring or rooting stem cuttings in moist perlite.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes — one of the best heat-loving annuals for attracting butterflies and hummingbirds.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in warm starting mix; transplant carefully as Tithonia dislikes root disturbance — use biodegradable pots if starting indoors. Mexican sunflower thrives in heat, poor soil, and dry conditions, making it ideal for back-of-border plantings in Gulf Coast summers when other annuals falter. The vivid orange, 3-inch daisy-like flowers are irresistible to monarch butterflies, swallowtails, and hummingbirds from midsummer through frost. Deadhead regularly by cutting stems (not just the flower head) back to a side shoot to maintain tidy plants and prolong heavy bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors 2–4 weeks before last frost after nicking or soaking seed; transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Nick or soak seeds in warm water for 12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep. Moonflower is a vigorous, fast-growing vine that needs a strong trellis or fence. Unlike morning glories, its large, fragrant white blossoms open in the evening and remain open through the night and into the following morning. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers to encourage blooming.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; or nick and soak seed overnight then start indoors 2–4 weeks early.

directions: Nick the hard seed coat with a file or soak in warm water for 8–12 hours before sowing 1/2 in deep to speed germination. Morning glories need a fence, trellis, or other support and are best sown directly at the base of their support. Avoid over-fertilizing, which promotes leaves at the expense of flowers. Blooms open in the morning and close by afternoon on each individual day.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 weeks before last frost; nasturtiums prefer cool soil and bolt in summer heat.

directions: Soak large seeds overnight then direct sow 1/2 in deep in poor, well-drained soil. Avoid fertilizing, as rich soil produces lush leaves at the expense of flowers. Deadhead spent blooms to prolong the season, or allow them to set seed and collect for next year. Both flowers and leaves are edible with a peppery flavor.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in fine-textured germination mix at 60–65°F; avoid higher temperatures as heat inhibits germination. Plant outdoors in cool weather in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade where summers are hot. Nemesia blooms prolifically in cool conditions with cheerful two-toned flowers in a huge range of colors; it typically falters in summer heat so shear back and move containers to a cooler spot. Plants may revive and rebloom in fall when temperatures drop again.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a damp paper towel in the refrigerator before sowing indoors to improve germination rates. Pinch plants back by one-third in late spring to encourage bushier growth and delay bloom until fall, when their purple flowers are invaluable for pollinators. Tall varieties may need staking or can be grown through a peony cage. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in early spring to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants bloom in year two from seed but transplants purchased in spring bloom the first year.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination. Plant in moist to average soil in full sun; established plants spread aggressively by underground rhizomes and may require annual division to prevent them from overtaking the border. The name refers to the curious behavior of individual florets staying in place when moved along the square stem. The pink to lilac spikes bloom in late summer and fall when pollinators are seeking food before winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil and germinate best when sown directly outdoors.

directions: Surface-sow seeds in early spring on well-prepared, well-drained soil; seeds need cool temperatures and light, so press firmly without covering. Oriental poppies bloom for a brief but spectacular 2–3 weeks in late spring to early summer with crepe-paper petals in orange, red, pink, or white up to 6 in across. Plants go dormant after bloom leaving a bare spot — interplant with late-emerging annuals like baby's breath or dahlias to fill the gap. Divide clumps in late summer while dormant; established plants dislike being moved during the growing season.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–8 weeks before the ground freezes, for late-spring to early-summer bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall at a depth of 3 times their diameter in sharply drained soil; the large-flowered types like 'Globemaster' can be planted 12 in deep for stability. Foliage often begins to yellow while the flowers are still in bloom — interplanting with perennials helps mask the dying leaves. Deer and rodents avoid alliums, making them a good choice for wildlife-prone gardens. Spherical seedheads are ornamentally interesting and can be dried for arrangements.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds in the dark (cover lightly and keep the tray in darkness) at 60–70°F; darkness improves germination for salpiglossis. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petunia-like flowers with intricate veining in yellow, red, purple, maroon, and bicolor thrive in cool weather. Provide light support with twiggy stakes since plants can flop in wind; pinch tips once for bushier plants. Plants decline in summer heat — treat them as a spring and fall flower in warm climates or plant where afternoon shade moderates temperatures.

planting.note: Start indoors 12–14 weeks before last frost for spring planting, or start in late summer for fall transplanting. Plants tolerate hard frost once established.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a moist starting mix; cover the tray with dark plastic as darkness aids germination. Transplant outdoors several weeks before the last frost, as cool weather brings out the richest colors and most abundant bloom. Deadhead spent flowers regularly to prevent seed set, which stops flowering. Plants decline as summer heat arrives; remove and replace with heat-tolerant annuals.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; seeds benefit from 4 weeks of cold stratification.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks in damp medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors for best germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained to dry soil. Penstemon thrives in poor, gravelly soil and is one of the most important native nectar plants for hummingbirds and native bees. The tubular white to pale pink flowers appear in late spring to early summer; deadhead to a basal rosette of leaves after bloom. Excellent drainage is critical — wet winter conditions are the leading cause of plant loss.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist fine-textured mix and provide light at 70–80°F; germination occurs within 7–14 days. Pentas is one of the best butterfly and hummingbird plants for hot, humid gardens, blooming continuously from transplant to frost without deadheading. Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil and fertilize monthly to sustain the heavy flower production. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy; they recover quickly in heat and resume flowering within 2–3 weeks.

planting.note: Plant bare-root divisions in fall 4–6 weeks before the ground freezes; eyes no deeper than 1–2 in below soil surface.

directions: Plant roots with the pink eyes no more than 1–2 in below the soil surface — planting too deeply is the most common reason peonies fail to bloom. Peonies require 6 weeks of temperatures below 40°F to set flower buds, so they need cold winters and are not suited to zones 9+. Expect minimal bloom the first 1–2 years as the plant establishes; mature plants can live 50+ years with little care. Cut foliage to the ground in fall to remove potential disease inoculum.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained soil; established plants are drought-tolerant.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil — perennial salvia is exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant once established and will rot in soggy conditions. Shear plants back by one-third to one-half after the first flush of deep violet-blue spikes fades to trigger a strong second (and sometimes third) bloom. The aromatic foliage deters deer and rabbits, making it a reliable choice for wildlife-challenged gardens. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix — do not cover, as petunias need light to germinate. Pinch transplants by one-third when planting to encourage branching. Deadhead or shear back leggy plants by half in midsummer to renew vigor and flush of bloom. Feed every two weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer for best performance.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in a well-drained mix; germination is reliable at 60–70°F and seedlings grow quickly. Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — scabiosa performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and fades in intense summer heat. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continuous bloom from early summer until fall; flowers are excellent for cutting with long, wiry stems and last 7–10 days in a vase. In zones 7–10 try sowing in fall for earlier spring bloom before heat sets in.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost after surface-sowing and cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, sterile mix and cold-stratify at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks to break dormancy, then move to 65°F for germination. Grow on at cool temperatures (55–60°F) since heat causes etiolation; transplant into partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. Primroses bloom best in cool, moist conditions — they are outstanding spring plants but typically go dormant in summer heat. Divide crowded clumps after bloom in spring and replant divisions in refreshed, compost-amended soil.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface after last frost when soil is warm; seed needs warmth and light to germinate.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of sandy or gritty soil and press lightly — do not cover seed, as light is needed for germination. Portulaca is one of the most drought- and heat-tolerant annuals available, thriving in poor, dry soil where other flowers fail. Blooms open in full sun and close at night or in cloudy weather; modern varieties stay open longer. Self-sows readily in warm climates.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–8 weeks improves germination rates significantly. Transplant after last frost into well-drained soil — established plants tolerate drought well. Flowers typically appear in year 2 from seed; deadhead spent blooms to extend the season or leave seedheads for birds. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed in spring.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep and maintain even moisture at 65–75°F until germination. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — good drainage is critical, especially in winter, as plants rot in waterlogged conditions. The torch-like flower spikes transition from red or orange in bud to yellow as the florets open, creating a two-toned effect that attracts hummingbirds throughout summer and fall. In zones 5–6 tie the leaves together and mulch deeply around the crown before the first hard freeze to protect the growing point.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; plants may bloom the first fall from an early sowing or establish and bloom their second year.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained soil; germination is fast and reliable. Plant in full sun in poor to average, very well-drained soil — rose campion thrives under dry, sunny conditions that would stress many perennials. The intensely magenta-crimson flowers glow against the woolly, silver-white felted stems and leaves, which are ornamental throughout the growing season. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years) but self-sow abundantly so colonies persist indefinitely; thin or transplant self-sown seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

planting.note: Plant in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained soil; established plants are extremely drought-tolerant.

directions: Plant in poor to average, sharply drained soil in full sun — Russian sage declines quickly in fertile or moist soil and is highly susceptible to root rot in clay. Cut plants back hard in early spring (to about 6 in) just as new basal growth emerges; do not cut back in fall as the woody framework helps protect the crown. The airy silver stems and lavender-blue flowers create a haze of color from midsummer through fall and are beloved by pollinators. Deer avoid this plant due to its strongly aromatic foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall; division is easily done by digging and separating rooted stems.

directions: Plant in any well-drained soil — even poor, gravelly soil suits sedum perfectly; overly rich or moist soil causes floppy, open-centered plants. Tall sedums may flop in midseason; pinch once in late spring or grow through a support ring to keep them upright. Leave the russet seedheads standing through winter as they are attractive and provide structure in the dormant garden. Divide every 3–4 years in spring by separating the fleshy crown sections.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist potting mix and keep at 60–70°F for germination; transplant after the last frost into well-drained soil. Shasta daisies often bloom lightly the first year from seed and fully the second year — named cultivars from division or plugs bloom more reliably in year 1. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage repeat blooming from midsummer into fall. Divide clumps every 2–3 years in spring, discarding the woody center and replanting vigorous outer sections.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist starting mix and do not cover — seed needs light to germinate. Pinch seedlings once to promote multiple spikes. Transplant to the garden a few weeks before the last frost, as snapdragons thrive in cool conditions and can tolerate a light freeze. After the primary spike blooms, cut it off to encourage a second flush from side branches.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination of seed.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks before sowing; established divisions establish more reliably than seed-grown plants and can be done in spring or fall. Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, average to rich soil; plants tolerate wet conditions and make excellent rain garden plants. The three-petaled flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white open only for a single morning but are produced in rapid succession over many weeks. Shear the entire plant to 8 in after the main bloom flush to stimulate fresh foliage and a second round of flowers in fall.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild frost.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in moist starting mix; seeds come in a papery casing and germination is slow. Statice prefers cool conditions early in the season. Harvest flower stems for drying when 75% of the florets on each branch have opened. The papery calyces — which hold their color dried — are the ornamental part; the true petals are tiny and brief.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as young plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix — light aids germination, so cover with only the thinnest layer of vermiculite if at all. Stock thrives in cool weather (55–65°F nights) and the fragrance is intensified by cool temperatures. Transplant to the garden in early spring; plants begin to decline when daytime temperatures consistently exceed 80°F. Cut flowers when one-third of the buds on the spike are open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant after last frost.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist medium at 35–40°F for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; germination is slow but improved by stratification. Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; Stokes' aster is native to the American Southeast and tolerates heat and humidity well but dislikes wet winter conditions. The large, cornflower-like blooms in blue, lavender, or white appear from early summer to fall and are excellent for cutting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly and are well-behaved in the border.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist starting mix and press lightly — light aids germination, so barely cover seed. Strawflowers tolerate drought and poor soils remarkably well. For drying, harvest stems when the central button of each head is still closed, before the outer bracts fully open; they will continue to open as they dry. Deadhead garden plants regularly to maintain continuous bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost when soil is warm; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for extended bloom.

directions: Direct sow seed 1 in deep after the last frost date, thinning to final spacing once seedlings reach 6 in tall. Sunflowers prefer poor-to-average soil and resent transplanting due to a taproot. Single-stemmed types bloom once; branching varieties produce multiple heads if cut regularly. Harvest seed heads when the back of the head turns yellow-brown and seeds feel firm.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; alyssum tolerates light frost and prefers cool weather.

directions: Scatter seed on the soil surface and press gently — light aids germination; just barely cover if needed to prevent the seed from blowing away. Sweet alyssum may slow or stop blooming in peak summer heat; shear plants back by half and they will rebound strongly when temperatures cool in early fall. The honey-scented flowers attract beneficial insects throughout the season.

planting.note: Direct sow outdoors 4–8 weeks before last frost; seeds prefer cool soil (55–65°F) and young plants tolerate frost.

directions: Nick or soak seeds overnight before sowing 1 in deep in cool, enriched soil at the base of a trellis. Sweet peas perform best during cool weather and decline as summer heat arrives — sow as early as possible. Cut flowers frequently, as even a few fading blooms left on the plant will trigger seed set and halt blooming. Provide sturdy support from the start, as vines climb to 4–6 ft.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant can go out 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants are frost-tolerant.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; sweet William is technically a biennial that flowers in its second year, but modern annual varieties bloom the first year from an early indoor start. The domed, fragrant flower clusters in vivid shades of red, pink, white, and bicolor appear in late spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters promptly to extend the blooming period; plants self-sow readily to maintain a colony from year to year.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 1–2 weeks; often blooms from seed the first year.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist towels for 1–2 weeks at 35–40°F, then sow indoors at 65–75°F; germination remains erratic over 14–28 days. Plant in full sun in poor to average, well-drained soil; this South American native is drought-tolerant and naturalizes readily once established. The wiry, branching stems carry clusters of small purple flowers from midsummer through frost at an unusual height that allows it to weave transparently through other plants. Grown as an annual north of zone 7, it self-sows prolifically even in cold climates and seedlings replace killed parent plants.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 8–11 it acts as a short-lived perennial and blooms from spring through hard frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in warm starting mix and transplant after frost danger has passed; tropical milkweed establishes quickly and blooms in its first season unlike most native milkweeds. It is an important monarch host plant and nectar source throughout the Gulf Coast growing season, but in zone 9–11 where it does not freeze back, cut plants to 6 in in fall and winter to disrupt the overwinter buildup of Ophryocystis elektroscirrha (OE), a protozoan parasite that accumulates on non-dormant milkweed. Deadhead spent flower clusters to encourage continuous blooming, or allow seedpods to dry and open for easy self-sowing. The red-and-yellow bicolor flowers distinguish it from native milkweeds at a glance.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall when soil temperatures drop below 55°F, about 4–8 weeks before hard frost.

directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up at a depth of 3 times their height, typically 6–8 in deep, in well-drained soil — standing water will cause rot. In warmer zones (7–8), pre-chill bulbs for 6–8 weeks in the refrigerator before planting to simulate the cold period they need. Most hybrid tulips perform best in year 1; replanting fresh bulbs annually is common practice in mild climates. Allow leaves to die back naturally after bloom to recharge the bulb.

planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost; an extremely tough Texas native that thrives in heat, part shade, and dry soils where few flowering shrubs will grow.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in partial to full sun; Turk's cap is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering natives for the Gulf South and performs well under the canopy of live oaks. The distinctive red flowers never fully open, forming a twisted turban shape, and are a top hummingbird nectar source from summer through fall. Small red fruits that follow are edible and mildly sweet — birds devour them quickly, and new plants sprout readily from seeds they drop. Prune back to 12–18 in in late winter; in zone 7–8 the top dies back but the roots are reliably hardy to about 10°F.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained soil; excellent drought tolerance once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun; speedwell is notably drought-tolerant and performs poorly in soggy or heavy clay conditions. The upright spikes of blue, pink, or white flowers bloom from late spring into summer and are excellent for cutting. Deadhead by removing spent spikes back to a lateral bud or leaf to extend the flowering period and prevent excessive self-seeding. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor and an attractive mounding habit.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant several weeks before last frost as plants tolerate mild freezes.

directions: Sow seed 1/8 in deep in a cool, moist starting mix; darkness during germination is beneficial. Violas are more petite and heat-tolerant than pansies, often self-sowing prolifically in the garden. Deadhead to extend flowering, or allow seed to set for naturalized self-seeding the following year. They shine in early spring containers and border edges.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost due to slow growth; transplant after frost danger has fully passed.

directions: Sow dust-fine seed on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright light — do not cover, as seed needs light to germinate. Start early as wax begonias are slow growers before transplant size. These self-cleaning plants rarely need deadheading, blooming continuously from spring through frost. Bronze-leaved cultivars tolerate more direct sun than green-leaved types.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix without covering; they need light to germinate at 70–75°F. Torenia is one of the best flowering annuals for shaded and part-shaded spots in hot, humid climates where impatiens are lost to downy mildew. The bicolored, snapdragon-like flowers in purple, pink, white, or yellow bloom profusely without deadheading throughout summer. Keep soil consistently moist, as plants wilt quickly when dry but recover readily; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid feed.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil temperature is above 65°F; also easy to start indoors 4–6 weeks early.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 in deep directly in the garden once frost risk is past and soil is warm. Pinch seedlings at 6 in tall to promote branching and a fuller plant with more stems for cutting. Cut flowers frequently to the next set of leaves to stimulate continuous bloom through summer. Avoid overhead watering to reduce powdery mildew; water at the base.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or grafted stock in spring after all frost danger; in zone 9 site against a south-facing wall for extra warmth.

directions: Acerola produces cherry-like fruits with one of the highest natural concentrations of vitamin C of any food plant — up to 65 times that of an orange. The shrubby tree is self-fertile and can bear multiple times per year in truly frost-free climates; it is very sensitive to cold below 30°F and root systems are damaged below 28°F. Plant in well-drained, slightly acid to neutral soil and fertilize lightly with a balanced fertilizer three times per year. Fruit ripens very quickly from green to red and is extremely perishable — harvest when fully red and process (freeze, juice) within 24–48 hours for maximum vitamin C retention.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter before bud break; almonds bloom very early and need a frost-free spring.

directions: Almonds bloom earlier than almost any other tree fruit and are severely damaged by late spring frosts, which is why commercial production is limited to Mediterranean-type climates. Most varieties require a cross-pollinator — plant at least two compatible cultivars (Non-Pareil with Carmel or Price, for example). Trees thrive in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soils and are drought-tolerant once established. Harvest when the hulls split open and the shells dry and harden, typically in late summer; spread nuts to dry for two weeks before storing.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; persimmons have a deep taproot so handle with care and disturb roots as little as possible.

directions: American persimmon is dioecious — male and female flowers occur on separate trees — so plant at least one of each for reliable fruiting, though some populations produce fruit without pollination. It is exceptionally cold-hardy and tolerant of poor soils, making it one of the easiest native fruit trees. Fruit is intensely astringent until fully ripe and soft, usually after the first hard freeze; pick after the flesh has turned translucent orange and jelly-soft. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead or crossing wood.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while still dormant, before buds break.

directions: Plant bare-root stock in well-drained soil, spreading roots carefully; stake dwarf trees for the first two seasons. Most varieties require a second cultivar nearby for cross-pollination — check chill-hour requirements to match your climate (600–1200 hours for standard types, under 400 for low-chill selections). Prune annually in late dormancy to an open-center or central-leader form to improve light penetration and air flow. Harvest when fruit separates with a gentle upward twist and seeds have turned brown, 2–5 years after planting.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter, as early as the soil can be worked, to allow root establishment before bloom.

directions: Most apricot varieties are self-fertile, which simplifies home-orchard planting, but two trees will increase yields. The key limiting factor in temperate climates is the tree's early bloom time — a hard frost after bud break will eliminate the crop, so choose a sheltered north-facing or slightly elevated site to delay bloom, or select late-blooming cultivars. Prune in early summer rather than winter to reduce risk of cytospora canker infection. Harvest when the fruit is fully orange, fragrant, and gives slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; in mild-winter zones plant in fall.

directions: Asian pears ripen on the tree — unlike European pears they do not need post-harvest ripening — so taste-test for sweetness before picking. Most varieties are self-unfruitful and yield best with a second Asian pear or even a compatible European variety nearby. Chill requirements are moderate (450–700 hours), making many selections suitable for zones 5–9. Annual thinning of fruit clusters to one per spur prevents limb breakage and encourages larger, better-flavored fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger has passed; fall planting is fine in zones 8–10.

directions: Asian persimmons come in astringent types (such as Hachiya, best eaten when jelly-soft) and non-astringent types (such as Fuyu, eaten firm like an apple). Most cultivars are self-fertile and produce well as single trees. Trees need very little pruning — maintain a central leader and remove inward-growing branches to open the canopy. In cooler parts of zone 7, choose hardier varieties and site against a south-facing wall; in zones 9–10, low-chill cultivars perform best.

planting.note: Plant in spring when all frost risk is past and soil is warm; avoid planting in summer heat.

directions: Avocados bear Type A flowers (open as female in the morning) and Type B flowers (open as female in the afternoon); planting one of each type near each other ensures pollen is available throughout the day and greatly increases fruit set. Grafted trees begin fruiting in 3–5 years; seedlings may take 8–10 years. Avocados are sensitive to root rot in heavy or poorly drained soils — plant on a mound or raised bed and irrigate deeply but infrequently. Fruit does not ripen on the tree; pick when full-sized and let ripen at room temperature for 3–8 days.

planting.note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; young trees are cold-sensitive and need protection until the trunk is at least 2 inches in diameter.

directions: Black sapote is a relative of the Asian persimmon; when ripe its flesh turns chocolate-brown and has a rich, pudding-like flavor often described as chocolate mousse. Trees are dioecious or polygamous — plant at least two seedlings or a grafted female with a male nearby for reliable fruiting. They prefer well-drained, fertile soils with a pH near 6.5 and are sensitive to frost, waterlogging, and salt winds. Harvest when the skin turns from bright olive-green to olive-yellow-green and the fruit gives to very gentle pressure; leave at room temperature 5–7 days until the inside is completely dark and custardy.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; site carefully as the tree grows very large and produces juglone.

directions: Black walnut is a native North American species grown primarily for its richly flavored nuts and valuable timber. It produces high concentrations of juglone in its roots, leaves, and hulls, which is toxic to many garden plants including tomatoes, peppers, apples, and blueberries; maintain a 50–80 foot buffer from sensitive plants. Trees are monoecious and partially self-fertile but cross well with a second tree. Hulls stain skin and clothing an indelible dark brown; wear gloves when husking. Nuts are ready when hulls are yellow-green to black and dent when pressed; cure for 2–3 weeks after husking.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; blood oranges require cool winters for anthocyanin color development — they color best in climates with cold nights during ripening.

directions: Blood orange cultivars (Moro, Tarocco, Sanguinello) develop their signature ruby-red flesh and juice through the accumulation of anthocyanins, a pigment triggered by cool nights during fruit development — temperatures between 40 and 50°F for several weeks from late autumn through midwinter produce the deepest coloring. In frost-free tropical climates the flesh remains orange with little anthocyanin. Trees are self-fertile, medium-sized, and managed identically to other sweet oranges: plant in well-drained soil, fertilize with a citrus blend three times per year, and irrigate deeply but infrequently. Harvest from late winter through early spring when the flesh has developed full color and the fruit is sweet.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; choose disease-resistant hybrid selections due to butternut canker pressure on the native species.

directions: Butternut, or white walnut, produces rich, oily nuts with a milder, sweeter flavor than black walnut and is native to eastern North America. The species is severely threatened by Sirococcus clavigignenti-juglandacearum (butternut canker), so seek out canker-resistant cultivars or hybrids (butternut x heartnut crosses, or selections from university breeding programs) rather than straight-species seedlings. Like other walnuts, butternut is monoecious and partially self-fertile but benefits from a second tree nearby; it also produces juglone that can inhibit nearby sensitive plants. Harvest when the sticky, hairy green hulls begin to soften and turn yellow-brown in early to mid-fall; hull promptly and dry nuts for 2–3 weeks before cracking or storing.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; calamondin is excellent for container culture in zones 7–8, where it can be brought indoors in winter.

directions: Calamondin is a hybrid between a kumquat and a mandarin orange, producing prolific small, sour-sweet fruits that are eaten whole like kumquats or used as a lime substitute in Southeast Asian cooking. The tree is self-fertile, nearly thornless, highly ornamental with fragrant white flowers, and among the easiest citrus to grow in containers — it tolerates indoor conditions better than most citrus. It tolerates brief dips to about 20°F when established. Fruit ripens from late autumn through spring; harvest when fully orange for best flavor, though the rind remains sweet while the flesh is tart even at the green-orange stage.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after all frost danger has passed; canistel is limited to truly frost-free zones in the continental U.S.

directions: Canistel is an evergreen tropical tree that produces smooth, orange-yellow fruit with dense, dry, egg-yolk-colored flesh — hence the common name egg fruit. Grafted trees are strongly preferred over seedlings; seedlings may take 5–7 years to fruit and show variable quality, while grafted plants can begin bearing in 2–3 years. The tree is drought-tolerant once established and tolerates a range of well-drained soils but is damaged by temperatures below 32°F even briefly. Harvest when the skin turns fully golden-yellow and yields firmly to pressure; ripen indoors at room temperature for 3–5 days until completely soft and sweet.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; young trees need protection from cold and wind for the first two seasons.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; carambola is sensitive to flooding and cold below 27°F even briefly, limiting outdoor cultivation to frost-free zones. The tree blooms multiple times per year and produces fruits along the trunk and main branches — a trait called cauliflory — so hang fruit-protection bags in commercial settings to prevent blemishes. Harvest when the ribs have turned fully yellow and the fruit has slight give; overripe fruit browns quickly at the tips. Fertilize with a balanced citrus-type fertilizer 3–4 times per year to sustain heavy bearing.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost; carob is well-suited to dry Mediterranean-climate zones 9–11 and tolerates alkaline, rocky soils.

directions: Carob is a slow-growing evergreen legume tree that fixes atmospheric nitrogen and thrives in dry, rocky, alkaline soils where few other fruits survive; it is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established. Trees are dioecious — plant one male for every 8–10 female trees, or choose hermaphroditic cultivars such as Santa Fe and Clifford that produce fruit without a separate male. The long, dark-brown seed pods ripen in late summer and fall and are left on the tree to dry and sweeten; they are harvested when fully brown, dry, and the seeds rattle inside. Carob pods are sweet and chocolatey and used ground as a cocoa substitute or in stock feed.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost danger; in coastal California (zone 10a) fall planting before the rainy season is ideal.

directions: Cherimoya thrives in California's coastal valleys where marine influence moderates temperature; it is less suited to the interior heat of zones 9–10a than to the mild, humid coast. Its flowers are protogynous — the stigma is receptive before the pollen is shed — so hand-pollinate by collecting pollen from partially open flowers in the afternoon and transferring it to newly opened receptive flowers in the morning for reliable fruit set. Trees are partially deciduous and shed leaves during dry or cool periods; withhold irrigation slightly during this dormancy to mimic the seasonally dry upland habitat. Harvest when the green skin begins to turn pale or slightly yellowish and the fruit gives under light thumb pressure; ripen at room temperature 3–5 days until soft and fragrant.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring; chestnuts strongly prefer acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Chestnuts require cross-pollination between at least two different cultivars or seedlings — plant them within 50 feet of each other for reliable nut set. American chestnut was devastated by blight, so choose blight-resistant Chinese, European, or hybrid selections (such as Dunstan Chestnuts). Trees strongly prefer acidic soils (pH 4.5–6.5) and perform poorly in alkaline or wet conditions. Harvest in fall when burs open and nuts fall; collect daily as nuts on the ground deteriorate quickly. Fresh chestnuts are perishable — refrigerate promptly and use or freeze within a month.

planting.note: Plant grafted or container-grown trees in spring after all frost risk has passed; in frost-free zones fall planting avoids summer heat stress.

directions: Guava is self-fertile and productive as a single tree; grafted selections of known cultivars (Ruby Supreme, Thai White) are superior to seedlings for flavor and bearing consistency. Trees tolerate a wide range of soils but are sensitive to standing water and prolonged frost — temperatures below 28°F damage foliage and below 24°F kill established trunks. Prune annually to 10–12 ft to simplify harvest and improve light penetration; guava bears on new growth so heading-back encourages productivity. Harvest when the skin softens, the color shifts from green to yellow or pink, and the fruit is intensely fragrant.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in late winter to early spring before bud break; fall planting also works in zones 5–7.

directions: Cornelian cherry is one of the earliest-blooming woody plants — small yellow flowers appear in late winter before the leaves — and the small, olive-shaped drupes ripen in late summer to a rich ruby red. Plant at least two seedlings or cultivars for best fruit set, as it is partially self-fertile but cross-pollination greatly improves yields. The tree is extremely cold-hardy and tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and urban pollution once established. Harvest when fruit is fully dark red and slightly soft; it is too tart to eat out of hand but makes outstanding preserves, syrups, and fruit leather.

planting.note: Plant offshoots or container stock in spring when the soil is warm; dates require the specific combination of desert heat and dry air to ripen properly.

directions: Dates are dioecious — you need a male tree to pollinate female trees, with one male sufficient for up to 50 females; commercial growers hand-pollinate by shaking male flower strands over female inflorescences in spring. Ripe dates require extremely hot, dry summers (110°F daytime heat preferred) with very low humidity during the ripening period — this limits quality fruit production in the continental U.S. almost entirely to the Coachella Valley and low desert Arizona. Offshoots from the base of female palms are the preferred planting material as they preserve the sex and fruit type of the parent; seed-grown palms yield 50% males and highly variable fruit. Harvest in fall when fruit reaches the desired stage: khalal (crisp, astringent), rutab (partly soft, sweet), or tamr (fully dried).

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; in mild-winter zones fall planting works well.

directions: English walnuts are monoecious — male (catkins) and female flowers occur on the same tree — and most cultivars are partially self-fertile, though a second tree improves yields. The tree produces juglone, a chemical that inhibits growth of many plants beneath its canopy; avoid planting near vegetables, berries, or sensitive ornamentals. Grafted Carpathian-strain trees are more cold-hardy and begin fruiting in 4–7 years; seedling trees may take 10+ years. Harvest in fall when hulls begin to crack and turn dark; remove husks promptly and cure nuts for 2–3 weeks before storing.

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root figs after the last frost date when soil has warmed; in mild climates, fall planting works well.

directions: Common figs (the type grown in most gardens) are parthenocarpic and self-fertile — no pollinator or fig wasp is needed. In zones 6–7 protect the trunk and lower branches over winter by wrapping with burlap or mounding mulch; established roots often resprout even after top-kill. Figs bear two crops on some cultivars: a small breba crop on old wood in early summer and a main crop on new growth in late summer. Harvest when the neck droops, the skin splits slightly, and the fruit is soft and fragrant; a drop of syrup at the eye signals perfect ripeness.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; requires the longest, hottest growing season of any common citrus.

directions: Grapefruit is self-fertile and produces best in the warmest citrus climates — the prolonged heat of south Texas and southern California delivers better sugar development than coastal or inland zones 9. Trees are large and vigorous; allow 20–25 feet between them and avoid overcrowding with other trees. Ruby Red and Flame are popular pink-fleshed varieties with good cold tolerance for zone 9. Fruit takes 12–18 months from bloom to full ripeness; taste-test from late autumn onward — the peel may remain slightly greenish even when the juice is sweet.

planting.note: Plant bare-root shrubs/trees in late winter to early spring; hazelnuts establish readily and tolerate a range of soils.

directions: Hazelnuts require cross-pollination and are wind-pollinated, so plant at least two different cultivars within 20 feet of each other; pollen is shed in late winter before leaves emerge. American hazelnut (C. americana) is hardier and more disease-resistant than European types; many gardeners grow hybrid selections for both traits. Eastern filbert blight (Anisogramma anomala) is a serious fungal disease in the eastern United States — choose blight-resistant cultivars such as Jefferson or York for eastern plantings. Harvest in late summer when nuts begin to fall from the husks on their own.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring; heartnut adapts to a wide range of soils and is one of the most productive walnuts for northern growers.

directions: Heartnut is a variety of Japanese walnut bearing distinctively heart-shaped nuts that crack open cleanly along a suture to release a whole kernel — unlike black walnut, which requires significant effort to extract meat. Trees are monoecious and partially self-fertile but yield better with a second tree; they begin bearing in 4–7 years from grafted stock. Heartnut is notably less allelopathic than black walnut, making it more garden-friendly as a specimen tree. Nuts fall in clusters in late summer to early fall; hull and dry for 2–3 weeks before cracking and storing.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after all frost risk passes; jaboticaba is extremely slow-growing and may take 8–15 years from seed to first fruit.

directions: Jaboticaba produces grape-like berries directly on its trunk and main branches (cauliflory) in dramatic clusters; the fruit ripens just 20–25 days after flowering and can bear several times per year in frost-free climates. It is self-fertile and requires well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.5); chlorosis is common in alkaline soils and can be corrected with chelated iron and sulfur. Trees are slow-growing, making them well-suited to container culture where they can be overwintered in zone 8 or moved under frost protection. Harvest berries when they are entirely deep purple-black, soft, and sweet-tart — they ferment within 3–4 days at room temperature, so refrigerate or process promptly.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date; jujubes establish quickly in warm soil.

directions: Jujube is exceptionally drought-tolerant and thrives in hot, dry climates where other fruits struggle; it prefers well-drained, even sandy soils and performs poorly with excessive moisture. Most cultivars are self-fertile, though a second variety increases yields. Li and Lang are the most widely planted in North America; Li produces large, round, date-like fruit while Lang is more elongated. Fruit is eaten fresh when crisp and apple-flavored or dried to a sweet, wrinkled date; harvest when skin turns from yellow-green to mahogany-red.

planting.note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; key lime is the most cold-sensitive common citrus and is suitable only for zones 10–11 in the ground.

directions: Key lime is a small, thorny tree distinct from the larger Persian lime (Citrus latifolia) — its fruit is smaller, more aromatic, more acidic, and nearly seedless at full yellow ripeness. It is self-fertile and bears prolifically in truly tropical conditions; the tree is damaged by any frost and requires zone 10–11 for outdoor culture, though it adapts well to large containers for zone 9 growers who can move it inside. Harvest fruit while still light green to yellow-green, before it turns fully yellow; yellow fruit is riper and less acid but also more perishable. Apply citrus fertilizer three times per year and ensure excellent drainage to prevent root rot.

planting.note: Plant in early spring before bud break; Korean pine is a cold-hardy alternative to Stone pine for producing large, commercially sized pine nuts in the northern U.S.

directions: Korean pine is a large, ornamental five-needle pine that produces the large, wingless pine nuts commonly sold in Asian markets — seeds are bigger than those of Italian stone pine and have a rich, buttery flavor. Trees are monoecious and bear cones that ripen over two growing seasons; significant cone production typically begins at 15–25 years from seed. Cones are harvested in early autumn when the scales begin to separate; spread cones on tarps to dry and then beat or tumble to dislodge seeds. The outer seed coat must be cracked to access the edible kernel, which is high in oil and should be refrigerated or frozen to prevent rancidity.

planting.note: Plant in spring; kumquats are the hardiest citrus relative and tolerate brief dips to 18°F.

directions: Kumquats are the hardiest of the citrus family and the only ones whose entire fruit — sweet peel and tangy flesh — is eaten whole. They are self-fertile and well-suited to container growing in zones 7–8, where they can be overwintered indoors. Unlike other citrus, kumquats go dormant more readily in winter, which improves their cold hardiness. Fruit ripens from late autumn through winter; harvest when fully orange and sweet-peel is prominent.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; in frost-free zones plant any time but avoid midsummer planting.

directions: Lemons are self-fertile and can bloom and fruit year-round in frost-free climates; Eureka and Lisbon are the commercial standards, while Meyer lemon is hardier (damaged below 22°F) and better suited to containers in cooler climates. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil and fertilize with citrus-specific fertilizer three times per year. In zones 8–9, grow in large containers and bring indoors when temperatures drop below 28°F. Fruit is ready when fully yellow, slightly soft, and the rind is thin — do not wait for it to fall off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; limes are the most cold-sensitive citrus.

directions: Key lime (C. aurantiifolia) is the most cold-sensitive common citrus and is reliable only in zones 10–11; Persian lime (C. latifolia) is somewhat hardier and suits zone 9 in sheltered spots. Both are self-fertile and produce almost continuously in warm climates with adequate water. Container culture in a large pot (25+ gallons) allows limes to be grown farther north and moved indoors during frost. Fruit is best harvested while still green and fully sized — if left to yellow on the tree it becomes dry and flavorless.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees after all cold weather has passed; young trees are cold-sensitive and need protection until the trunk is well established.

directions: Plant grafted trees in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) in full sun; longan will not tolerate standing water or saturated roots. A brief winter cool-dry period — temperatures in the mid-40s°F for several weeks — is essential to trigger reliable flowering, making coastal south Florida and extreme south Texas the two viable zones in the continental United States. Fruit clusters ripen in summer and should be harvested by cutting whole panicles with scissors when the skin turns tan to light brown and the flesh is translucent and sweet. Prune annually after harvest to keep the canopy to a manageable height and promote new fruiting wood.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk has passed; in zones 9–11 fall planting is preferred.

directions: Loquat is an evergreen tree that flowers in fall and winter and ripens its fruit in late winter to early spring — one of the earliest fruiting trees of the season. Most varieties are self-fertile, though two trees increase crop size. Fruit clusters can be thinned at golf-ball size to produce fewer but larger fruit. Harvest when fruit is fully golden-yellow to orange, slightly soft, and sweet-tart; the thin skin is edible but seeds are large and should be discarded. Trees are cold-hardy to about 12°F but flowers and developing fruit are damaged below 28°F.

planting.note: Plant in spring after all frost risk has passed; in zones 10–11, fall planting after summer heat breaks is preferred.

directions: Plant a grafted or air-layered tree in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.5) in a site sheltered from salt spray and drying winter wind; lychee cannot tolerate waterlogged roots at any age. Trees require a cool-dry stress period of 4–6 weeks in late autumn — nights reliably below 59°F — to trigger flower bud development, so hold irrigation during that window rather than watering on a regular schedule. Fruit clusters develop rapidly after bloom and benefit from thinning to 3–5 fruits per panicle when marble-sized to improve final fruit size and reduce limb stress. Harvest clusters when the rough, bumpy skin has turned fully red to pinkish-red and the aroma is distinctly floral and sweet, typically 90–112 days after bloom.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost; macadamias grow best in frost-free zones with well-distributed rainfall.

directions: Macadamias are self-fertile but cross-pollination between two or more varieties meaningfully increases yields; grafted trees start bearing at 3–5 years, while seedlings can take 10–15 years. The tree prefers well-drained, slightly acid soil (pH 5.5–6.5) and is sensitive to root disturbance — plant without disturbing the root ball. Trees produce racemes of cream-white flowers that fall and create a slippery surface; harvesting is done by collecting nuts that have dropped naturally as the green husk splits and the nut falls. Shell and dry nuts for 3–4 weeks before cracking; the shells require a commercial or heavy-duty nut cracker as they are among the hardest of any food nut.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost; mandarins are among the hardiest citrus, tolerating brief dips to 24°F.

directions: Mandarins (including tangerines, clementines, and satsumas) are the most cold-hardy of the common sweet citrus, with satsumas surviving to about 15°F and thriving in zone 8. Most are self-fertile, though some varieties set more fruit with a compatible pollinator nearby. Fruit tends to ripen earlier in the season than oranges — from October through January depending on variety — and the loose peel and sweet-tart flavor make them popular for fresh eating. Avoid overwatering, as mandarins are somewhat drought-tolerant and susceptible to root rot in wet soils.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after all frost risk has passed; in zones 10b–11 the rainy-season break in early spring is the optimal planting window.

directions: Plant grafted trees of named cultivars (Kent, Tommy Atkins, Haden, Keitt) rather than seedlings; grafted trees fruit in 3–5 years and produce fruit true to type. Mangoes require a cool-dry period of 4–6 weeks during winter — nights below 60°F without frost — to trigger uniform flowering; excess rainfall during bloom causes anthracnose and poor fruit set. Prune after harvest each year by removing one-quarter to one-third of the outer canopy to keep the tree at a manageable height and promote fresh fruiting wood. Fruit is ripe when the shoulders near the stem fill out, color develops according to the cultivar, and the flesh yields slightly to pressure; the stem end is often the last to color.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while dormant; fall planting suits zones 6–8.

directions: Medlar is a small, gnarled tree that tolerates a wide range of soils and needs minimal pruning beyond removing crossing branches; it is largely self-fertile and rarely needs a pollinator. Fruit is harvested in late autumn after the first light frost when the skin has turned brown and slightly wrinkled, then 'bleted' (stored at room temperature for 2–4 weeks) until the flesh softens to a custard-like consistency and loses its harsh astringency. The bletted fruit tastes of apple butter with wine overtones and is traditionally made into jelly or cheese. Few pests bother medlar, making it one of the easiest heritage fruits for a low-spray garden.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; Meyer lemon is the hardiest true-fruiting lemon and can be grown in containers in zones 7–8 with winter protection.

directions: Meyer lemon is a natural hybrid between a lemon and either a mandarin or sweet orange, resulting in a thinner-skinned, less acidic, and more cold-tolerant fruit than standard lemon varieties; it tolerates brief dips to about 22°F once established. It is self-fertile, blooms nearly year-round in frost-free climates, and is exceptionally well-suited to container culture — a 20-gallon pot allows the plant to be moved indoors in zones 7–8. Plant in fast-draining citrus mix or well-amended garden soil and fertilize with citrus-specific fertilizer in late winter, late spring, and midsummer. Fruit is ripe when the skin turns deep golden-yellow and yields to gentle pressure; the rind is thin and zests well for culinary use.

planting.note: Sow seed or plant cuttings after last frost; moringa grows explosively in heat and can be coppiced to manage size in zone 9.

directions: Sow seeds 1 in deep directly into warm, well-drained soil — moringa dislikes transplanting due to its long taproot. It grows extraordinarily fast in hot, dry conditions and tolerates poor, sandy soils; avoid over-watering or heavy clay, which causes stem and root rot. In zone 9 the tree may freeze to the ground in hard winters but regrows vigorously from the root crown each spring. Harvest young leaves, flowers, and immature seed pods (drumsticks) regularly; coppicing the main stem at 3–4 ft encourages dense, bushy regrowth with more leaf area at reachable height.
sources: ECHO (Educational Concerns for Hunger Organization) technical note

planting.note: Plant in early spring; mulberries establish readily from container stock.

directions: Red mulberry (native to eastern North America) and white mulberry (Morus alba, from Asia) are both grown for fruit; white mulberry is more tolerant of heat and alkaline soils. Most mulberries are self-fertile or near-enough so that a single tree crops well. Trees require very little care once established — no regular spraying or thinning is needed. Fruit ripens over several weeks and drops when ripe; lay a tarp beneath the canopy for easy collection, as the juice stains pavement and clothing permanently.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring before bud break.

directions: Nectarines are essentially smooth-skinned peaches and share the same culture — self-fertile, open-center pruning, and annual heading-back of one-year-old wood. Because nectarines lack the peach's fuzzy skin barrier they are somewhat more susceptible to brown rot and bacterial spot in humid climates, so good air circulation is critical. Chill requirements range from 250 to 900 hours depending on cultivar; select accordingly. Fruit is ready when it gives slightly to thumb pressure and the background color has shifted from green to creamy yellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost; in Mediterranean-climate zones fall planting is also suitable.

directions: Olives thrive in the classic Mediterranean climate — hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters — and are extremely drought-tolerant once established, but they require 200–400 chill hours to set fruit reliably. Most cultivars are self-fertile, but cross-pollination with a second variety improves yields significantly. Trees are long-lived and slow-growing; minimal pruning is needed beyond thinning for light penetration. For table olives, harvest when fruit is still green-to-straw colored; for oil, pick when about half the fruit has turned black.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost risk passes; in frost-free zones, spring or early fall planting is ideal.

directions: Sweet oranges (Citrus sinensis) are self-fertile; navel oranges and many blood oranges are seedless because they are parthenocarpic. Most oranges need warm nights to develop sweetness and cannot be grown outdoors north of zone 9; the Hamlin and Navel types are slightly more cold-tolerant. Fruit can stay on the tree for weeks to months after ripening, extending harvest, but may re-green in warm weather without losing quality. Apply a balanced citrus fertilizer in late winter, late spring, and midsummer for best production.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all cold risk has passed; even a light frost kills papaya plants.

directions: Papaya is one of the fastest-bearing fruit plants — grafted or transplanted seedlings can fruit within 6–12 months of planting. It is highly sensitive to frost, waterlogged soil, and root disturbance; plant in well-raised, excellently drained beds and never let roots sit in water even briefly. Most commercial papaya is hermaphroditic (self-fertile), allowing a single tree to produce fruit; dioecious types require a male plant within 20 feet. Cut the growing tip back at 3–4 ft to encourage a compact, easily harvested form. Harvest when at least one-quarter of the skin has turned yellow; ripen fully at room temperature and refrigerate only after fully ripe.

planting.note: Plant container-grown pawpaws in late spring after frost; avoid bare-root stock as the taproot is easily damaged.

directions: Pawpaws require two genetically distinct seedlings or named cultivars for cross-pollination; hand-pollinating flowers with a small brush greatly improves fruit set. Young trees need shade for the first one to two seasons — plant under a light canopy or use a shade cloth — but mature trees prefer full sun to fruit well. The taproot makes transplanting difficult, so use container-grown stock and disturb roots as little as possible. Fruit ripens in late summer to fall and goes from firm to custardy-soft within days; refrigerate immediately after picking as shelf life is very short.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter before bud swell; in zones 8–9 fall planting is also successful.

directions: Most peaches are self-fertile, so a single tree will produce fruit, but they need 600–1000 chill hours — choose low-chill varieties (below 400 hours) for zones 8–9. Train to an open-center (vase) shape to allow sunlight deep into the canopy, and prune out about one-third of last year's growth each dormant season because peaches only fruit on one-year-old wood. Thin fruit to 6–8 inches apart after June drop to size up remaining fruit and reduce limb breakage. Harvest when the green ground color fades to cream and the fruit yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring while dormant; fall planting works in zones 7–9.

directions: European pears require 700–1200 chill hours and almost always need a second compatible cultivar for adequate pollination; Bosc and Anjou cross well with each other. Pears are prone to fire blight, so choose resistant varieties and prune out any infected wood well below the canker margin. Train to a central leader and avoid excess nitrogen, which promotes lush growth susceptible to blight. Pick pears while still firm and ripen indoors at room temperature — they go mealy if left on the tree too long.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter to early spring; pecans have a deep taproot — handle carefully and plant immediately.

directions: Pecans are protandrous or protogynous depending on variety — pollen shed and receptive female flowers rarely overlap on the same tree — so always plant at least two cultivars of opposite types (Type I with Type II) to ensure cross-pollination. Northern varieties (Hark, Kanza, Lakota) are bred for shorter growing seasons and suit zones 5–7; southern varieties need 250+ frost-free days.
